| 1 | Subgraph $H \subseteq G$ | $V' \subseteq V$ | $\vert E' \vert \leq \vert E \vert$ | $H$ may not span all vertices |
| 2 | Spanning Subgraph | $V' = V$ | $\vert E' \vert \leq \vert E \vert$ | Same vertex set as $G$ |
| 3 | Induced Subgraph $\langle V' \rangle$ | $V' \subseteq V$ | All $E$ within $V'$ | Unique induced subgraph |
| 4 | Union $G_1 \cup G_2$ | $V_1 \cup V_2$ | $\vert E_1 \vert + \vert E_2 \vert - \vert E_1 \cap E_2 \vert$ | Commutative |
| 5 | Intersection $G_1 \cap G_2$ | $V_1 \cap V_2$ | $\vert E_1 \cap E_2 \vert$ | Commutative |
| 6 | Complement $\bar{G}$ | $V$ | $\binom{n}{2} - \vert E \vert$ | $G \cup \bar{G} = K_n$ |
| 7 | Ring Sum $G_1 \oplus G_2$ | $V_1 \cup V_2$ | $\vert E_1 \vert + \vert E_2 \vert - 2\vert E_1 \cap E_2 \vert$ | Symmetric difference |
| 8 | Deletion $G - v$ | $V \setminus \{v\}$ | $\vert E \vert - \deg(v)$ | Vertex removal |
| 9 | Deletion $G - e$ | $V$ | $\vert E \vert - 1$ | Edge removal |
| 10 | Join $G_1 + G_2$ | $V_1 \cup V_2$ | $\vert E_1 \vert + \vert E_2 \vert + \vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert V_2 \vert$ | Requires disjoint graphs |
| 11 | Cartesian Product $G_1 \square G_2$ | $V_1 \times V_2$ | $\vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert E_2 \vert + \vert V_2 \vert \cdot \vert E_1 \vert$ | Commutative |
| 12 | Composition $G_1[G_2]$ | $V_1 \times V_2$ | $\vert V_2 \vert^2 \cdot \vert E_1 \vert + \vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert E_2 \vert$ | Non-commutative |

## 2.11 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of Graph Operations:**
> - **Network Design**: Union of subnetworks; join models for fully interconnected clusters.
> - **VLSI Layout**: Cartesian product models grid-based chip routing ($P_m \square P_n$).
> - **Social Networks**: Complement reveals "non-friends" suggestions; intersection finds mutual connections.
> - **Database Querying**: Subgraphs model sub-relations; ring sum finds exclusive relationships.
> - **Compiler Optimization**: Cartesian products represent loop-nest structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Complete Operation Analysis on $P_3$ and $K_2$

Let us take two small graphs and compute all key operations step-by-step.

### Setup
Let:
$$G_1 = P_3 = V = \{a, b, c\}, \, E = \{ab, bc\}$$

$$G_2 = K_2 = V = \{x, y\}, \, E = \{xy\}$$

### Step 1: Union $G_1 \cup G_2$

$$V(G_1 \cup G_2) = \{a, b, c, x, y\}$$

$$E(G_1 \cup G_2) = \{ab, bc, xy\}$$

**Edge count verification using formula**:

$$|E_1 \cup E_2| = |E_1| + |E_2| - |E_1 \cap E_2| = 2 + 1 - 0 = 3$$

### Step 2: Intersection $G_1 \cap G_2$

Since $G_1$ and $G_2$ have disjoint vertex sets, the intersection is empty:

$$G_1 \cap G_2 = (V_1 \cap V_2, \, E_1 \cap E_2) = (\varnothing, \, \varnothing)$$

### Step 3: Complement of $G_1$

For $G_1 = P_3$ on $n = 3$ vertices, total possible edges = $\binom{3}{2} = 3$.

$$|E(\bar{G_1})| = 3 - 2 = 1$$

$$\bar{G_1} = (V, \{ac\})$$

This single edge $ac$ connects the two endpoints, making $\bar{P_3} \cong K_1 \cup K_2$ (disjoint union).

### Step 4: Ring Sum $G_1 \oplus G_2$

$$E(G_1 \oplus G_2) = E_1 \triangle E_2 = (E_1 \cup E_2) \setminus (E_1 \cap E_2) = \{ab, bc, xy\}$$

Since $E_1 \cap E_2 = \varnothing$, the ring sum equals the union in this case.

### Step 5: Join $G_1 + G_2$

For join, we require disjoint graphs (which $G_1$ and $G_2$ are).

$$V(G_1 + G_2) = \{a, b, c, x, y\}$$

$$E(G_1 + G_2) = \{ab, bc, xy\} \cup E^*$$

where $E^*$ contains all edges between $\{a,b,c\}$ and $\{x,y\}$:

$$E^* = \{ax, ay, bx, by, cx, cy\}$$

**Total edges verification**:

$$|E(G_1 + G_2)| = 2 + 1 + (3 \times 2) = 9$$

Indeed, since $|V_1| \cdot |V_2| = 6$, total edges = $2 + 1 + 6 = 9$. ✓

### Step 6: Cartesian Product $G_1 \square G_2$

Vertices: $V_1 \times V_2 = \{(a,x), (a,y), (b,x), (b,y), (c,x), (c,y)\}$ — total **6 vertices**.

Edges formed by the rule:
- For each $u_1 u_2 \in E_1$: edges $(u_1, x)(u_2, x)$ and $(u_1, y)(u_2, y)$.
  - From $ab$: $(a,x)(b,x)$, $(a,y)(b,y)$
  - From $bc$: $(b,x)(c,x)$, $(b,y)(c,y)$
- For each $v_1 v_2 \in E_2$: edges $(a, v_1)(a, v_2)$, $(b, v_1)(b, v_2)$, $(c, v_1)(c, v_2)$.
  - From $xy$: $(a,x)(a,y)$, $(b,x)(b,y)$, $(c,x)(c,y)$

**Total edges verification using formula**:

$$|E(G_1 \square G_2)| = |V_1| \cdot |E_2| + |V_2| \cdot |E_1| = 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 7$$

### Step 7: Composition $G_1[G_2]$

Vertices: same as Cartesian product — $\{(a,x), (a,y), (b,x), (b,y), (c,x), (c,y)\}$.

Adjacency rule:
- If $u_1 u_2 \in E_1$: $(u_1, *)$ is adjacent to $(u_2, *)$ for **any** second coordinate.
- If $u_1 = u_2$ and $v_1 v_2 \in E_2$: edges within the same "copy" of $G_2$.

**Edge count verification using formula**:

$$|E(G_1[G_2])| = |V_2|^2 \cdot |E_1| + |V_1| \cdot |E_2| = 2^2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 = 8 + 3 = 11$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Edge Count of Complement

**Theorem**: For a simple graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, $|E(\bar{G})| = \binom{n}{2} - |E(G)|$.

**Proof**:

Let $E$ be the edge set of $G$. The complete graph $K_n$ contains all $\binom{n}{2}$ possible edges among $n$ vertices. By definition, $\bar{G}$ contains all edges of $K_n$ that are **not** in $G$.

$$\begin{aligned}
|E(\bar{G})| &= |E(K_n) \setminus E(G)| \\
&= |E(K_n)| - |E(G) \setminus E(K_n)| \\
&= |E(K_n)| - |E(G)| \quad (\text{since } E(G) \subseteq E(K_n)) \\
&= \binom{n}{2} - |E(G)|
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore |E(G)| + |E(\bar{G})| = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Connectivity Behavior Under Operations

| Operation | Effect on Connectivity | Effect on Regularity | Effect on Bipartiteness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subgraph | May disconnect | May become irregular | Preserved |
| Union | Combines connected components | Generally irregular | Preserved if both bipartite |
| Intersection | Often disconnected | Depends | Preserved |
| Complement | Connected if $|V| \geq 5$ (mostly) | $k$-regular $\Leftrightarrow$ $(n-1-k)$-regular | Generally NOT preserved |
| Ring Sum | Disconnected if disjoint | Depends | Preserved if both bipartite |
| Deletion of vertex | May disconnect | Affects degrees | Preserved |
| Deletion of edge | May disconnect | Reduces degree of 2 vertices | Preserved |
| Join | Always connected | Generally irregular | Generally NOT bipartite |
| Cartesian Product | Connected iff both connected | Product of degrees | Preserved |
| Composition | Connected iff $G_1$ connected | Generally irregular | Generally NOT preserved |

---

## 3.4 Verification Example: Complement Pair Identity

**Claim**: For any simple graph $G$, the complement of $\bar{G}$ is $G$ itself.

**Proof**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{\bar{G}} &= (V, \overline{E(\bar{G})}) \\
&= (V, E(K_n) \setminus E(\bar{G})) \\
&= (V, E(K_n) \setminus (E(K_n) \setminus E(G))) \\
&= (V, E(G)) \\
&= G
\end{aligned}$$

The second equality follows from the definition of complement, and the third uses set-theoretic double-complementation: $A \setminus (B \setminus A) = A$.

**Q.E.D.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the complement of a graph. If $G$ is a graph with 8 vertices and 12 edges, find the number of edges in $\bar{G}$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> The complement of a graph $G = (V, E)$, denoted $\bar{G}$, is a graph on the same vertex set $V$ such that two distinct vertices are adjacent in $\bar{G}$ if and only if they are **not adjacent** in $G$.

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
|E(\bar{G})| &= \binom{n}{2} - |E(G)| \\
&= \binom{8}{2} - 12 \\
&= 28 - 12 \\
&= 16
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore, $\bar{G}$ has $\boxed{16}$ edges.**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the ring sum of two graphs? State any two properties of the ring sum operation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The ring sum of two graphs $G_1 = (V_1, E_1)$ and $G_2 = (V_2, E_2)$ is defined as:

$$G_1 \oplus G_2 = (G_1 \cup G_2) - (G_1 \cap G_2)$$

The edge set is given by:

$$E(G_1 \oplus G_2) = E_1 \triangle E_2 = (E_1 \cup E_2) \setminus (E_1 \cap E_2)$$

**Two Properties (1 Mark):**
1. **Commutativity**: $G_1 \oplus G_2 = G_2 \oplus G_1$
2. **Associativity**: $G_1 \oplus (G_2 \oplus G_3) = (G_1 \oplus G_2) \oplus G_3$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Consider the graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ defined below:
- $G_1$: $V_1 = \{a, b, c, d\}$, $E_1 = \{ab, bc, cd, ad\}$
- $G_2$: $V_2 = \{c, d, e, f\}$, $E_2 = \{cd, de, ef, cf\}$

Find:
1. (i) $G_1 \cup G_2$
2. (ii) $G_1 \cap G_2$
3. (iii) $G_1 \oplus G_2$

**(b)** For the same graphs, compute $G_1 + G_2$ (join) and find the total number of edges. Justify using the formula.

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii) [7 Marks Total]

**(i) Union $G_1 \cup G_2$ (2 Marks):**

$$V(G_1 \cup G_2) = V_1 \cup V_2 = \{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$$

$$E(G_1 \cup G_2) = E_1 \cup E_2 = \{ab, bc, cd, ad, de, ef, cf\}$$

Total edges: $\vert E_1 \cup E_2 \vert = \vert E_1 \vert + \vert E_2 \vert - \vert E_1 \cap E_2 \vert = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$

**(ii) Intersection $G_1 \cap G_2$ (2 Marks):**

$$V(G_1 \cap G_2) = V_1 \cap V_2 = \{c, d\}$$

$$E(G_1 \cap G_2) = E_1 \cap E_2 = \{cd\}$$

(Taking only the common edge; the edge $cd$ appears in both graphs.)

**(iii) Ring Sum $G_1 \oplus G_2$ (3 Marks):**

$$V(G_1 \oplus G_2) = V_1 \cup V_2 = \{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$$

$$E(G_1 \oplus G_2) = (E_1 \cup E_2) \setminus (E_1 \cap E_2) = \{ab, bc, ad, de, ef, cf\}$$

The edge $cd$ is removed (as it is common), and all other edges are retained.

### Part (b) — Join Operation [7 Marks]

Since $G_1$ and $G_2$ share vertices $\{c, d\}$, the join requires us to consider them on a **common vertex set** or treat them as a single graph context. For the join operation, we take the **union** of vertices and edges, plus all cross edges between distinct vertex sets.

**Effective disjoint decomposition**: Treat the shared vertices separately.

$$V(G_1 + G_2) = V_1 \cup V_2 = \{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$$

$$E(G_1 + G_2) = E_1 \cup E_2 \cup E^*$$

where $E^*$ are the cross edges. However, since $c, d$ are shared and already connected to $a, b$ in $G_1$ and to $e, f$ in $G_2$, we need only add edges between vertices **uniquely** in $V_1$ and uniquely in $V_2$:

$$E^* = \{ae, af, be, bf\}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
|E(G_1 + G_2)| &= |E_1| + |E_2| + |V_1 \setminus V_2| \cdot |V_2 \setminus V_1| \\
&= 4 + 4 + 2 \cdot 2 \\
&= 12
\end{aligned}$$

The complete edge set is:
$$E(G_1 + G_2) = \{ab, bc, cd, ad, de, ef, cf, ae, af, be, bf, \text{extra shared edges}\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For disjoint graphs, $|E(G_1 + G_2)| = |E_1| + |E_2| + |V_1| \cdot |V_2|$. When graphs share vertices, we apply the modified formula accounting for overlaps.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the Cartesian product of two graphs. If $G_1 = P_3$ (path on 3 vertices) and $G_2 = K_2$ (complete graph on 2 vertices), construct $G_1 \square G_2$ and find its number of edges.

**(b)** Define the composition of two graphs. Compute the number of edges in $G_1[G_2]$ for the same graphs. Explain why $G_1[G_2] \neq G_2[G_1]$.

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Cartesian Product [7 Marks]

**Definition (3 Marks):**
The Cartesian product $G_1 \square G_2$ of two graphs $G_1 = (V_1, E_1)$ and $G_2 = (V_2, E_2)$ is defined as:
- **Vertex set**: $V(G_1 \square G_2) = V_1 \times V_2$
- **Edge rule**: Two vertices $(u_1, v_1)$ and $(u_2, v_2)$ are adjacent if either:
  - $u_1 = u_2$ and $v_1 v_2 \in E_2$, OR
  - $v_1 = v_2$ and $u_1 u_2 \in E_1$

**Construction (3 Marks):**
Let $V_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$ (with edges $12, 23$) and $V_2 = \{x, y\}$ (with edge $xy$).

Vertex set of $G_1 \square G_2$:
$$\{(1,x), (1,y), (2,x), (2,y), (3,x), (3,y)\}$$

Edges:
- From $G_2$ edge $xy$: $(1,x)(1,y)$, $(2,x)(2,y)$, $(3,x)(3,y)$ — **3 edges**
- From $G_1$ edge $12$: $(1,x)(2,x)$, $(1,y)(2,y)$ — **2 edges**
- From $G_1$ edge $23$: $(2,x)(3,x)$, $(2,y)(3,y)$ — **2 edges**

**Edge count (1 Mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
|E(G_1 \square G_2)| &= |V_1| \cdot |E_2| + |V_2| \cdot |E_1| \\
&= 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 \\
&= 3 + 4 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b) — Composition [7 Marks]

**Definition (3 Marks):**
The composition $G_1[G_2]$ of $G_1$ and $G_2$ is defined as:
- **Vertex set**: $V(G_1[G_2]) = V_1 \times V_2$
- **Edge rule**: Two vertices $(u_1, v_1)$ and $(u_2, v_2)$ are adjacent if either:
  - $u_1 u_2 \in E_1$ (regardless of $v_1, v_2$), OR
  - $u_1 = u_2$ and $v_1 v_2 \in E_2$

**Edge count calculation (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
|E(G_1[G_2])| &= |V_2|^2 \cdot |E_1| + |V_1| \cdot |E_2| \\
&= 2^2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 \\
&= 8 + 3 = 11
\end{aligned}$$

**Non-commutativity explanation (1 Mark):**
> [!NOTE]
> $G_1[G_2]$ creates **complete bipartite-like connections** between copies of $G_2$ whenever $u_1 u_2 \in E_1$. For example, since $12 \in E_1$, every vertex $(1, *)$ is adjacent to every vertex $(2, *)$, giving $2 \times 2 = 4$ edges between the first and second "copy" alone. In $G_2[G_1]$, the copies would be of size 3 each, leading to $3 \times 3 = 9$ edges for the same $E_2$ edge. Hence $G_1[G_2] \neq G_2[G_1]$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> **1. Complement Confusion ($-2$ marks):**
> Students often forget that the complement requires $G$ to be a **simple graph**. Loops and parallel edges must be handled separately, and the formula $|E(\bar{G})| = \binom{n}{2} - |E(G)|$ applies **only** to simple graphs.
> 
> **2. Ring Sum vs Union ($-1$ mark):**
> Ring sum is **NOT** the same as union. It excludes common edges. A common error is writing $G_1 \oplus G_2 = G_1 \cup G_2$ in general.
> 
> **3. Join Requires Disjoint Graphs ($-2$ marks):**
> The join $G_1 + G_2$ is strictly defined for **vertex-disjoint** graphs. When vertices overlap, students must clearly state the assumption before applying the formula $|E_1| + |E_2| + |V_1| \cdot |V_2|$.
> 
> **4. Cartesian Product Adjacency Rule ($-3$ marks):**
> Many students incorrectly write the OR condition as AND, or miss the cross-coordinate requirement entirely. Always verify: $(u_1, v_1)$ and $(u_2, v_2)$ are adjacent iff **(same row, column edge in $G_2$) OR (same column, row edge in $G_1$)**.
> 
> **5. Composition vs Cartesian Product Mix-up ($-3$ marks):**
> These two products have **different adjacency rules**. In Cartesian product, two copies are connected only via "matched" coordinates. In composition, entire copies are fully linked.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Operations on Graphs:**

- **Subgraph**: $H \subseteq G$ means $V(H) \subseteq V(G)$ and $E(H) \subseteq E(G)$.
- **Spanning Subgraph**: Same vertex set, subset of edges.
- **Induced Subgraph**: All edges of $G$ between chosen vertices are present.
- **Complement**: $\bar{G}$ on $n$ vertices has $\binom{n}{2} - |E|$ edges.
- **Union**: $|E_1 \cup E_2| = |E_1| + |E_2| - |E_1 \cap E_2|$.
- **Intersection**: Edges common to both, on common vertex pairs.
- **Ring Sum**: Edges in exactly one of $G_1$ or $G_2$; same as set symmetric difference.
- **Vertex Deletion $G - v$**: Removes $v$ and **all incident edges**.
- **Edge Deletion $G - e$**: Removes only the edge $e$; vertices unchanged.
- **Join $G_1 + G_2$**: Union plus **all cross edges** between $V_1$ and $V_2$; requires disjointness.
- **Cartesian Product $G_1 \square G_2$**: 
  - $|V| = |V_1| \cdot |V_2|$, $|E| = |V_1| \cdot |E_2| + |V_2| \cdot |E_1|$.
  - **Commutative**.
  - Adjacency: same first coord + edge in $G_2$, OR same second coord + edge in $G_1$.
- **Composition $G_1[G_2]$**:
  - $|V| = |V_1| \cdot |V_2|$, $|E| = |V_2|^2 \cdot |E_1| + |V_1| \cdot |E_2|$.
  - **NOT commutative**.
  - Adjacency: edge in $G_1$ between first coords (any second coords), OR same first coord + edge in $G_2$.
- **Double complement**: $\overline{\bar{G}} = G$.
- **Universal graph identity**: $G \cup \bar{G} = K_n$ (complete graph).
- **Always state disjointness assumption** when applying join or product formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hamiltonian Paths and Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hamiltonian Path — Formal Definition

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a simple undirected (or directed) graph. A **Hamiltonian Path** is a path that visits **every vertex of the graph exactly once**. 

Mathematically, a Hamiltonian path in $G$ is a sequence of distinct vertices $v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n$ such that for every $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$, the edge $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E(G)$.

If, in addition, the edge $(v_1, v_n) \in E(G)$, then the path closes into a **Hamiltonian Circuit** (or **Hamiltonian Cycle**), and we have a closed walk that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the starting vertex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> A Hamiltonian circuit is a closed walk of length $n$ (one that visits $n$ vertices and returns to the start), passing through each vertex of $G$ exactly **once**.

## 1.2 The Historical Analogy — Sir William Rowan Hamilton (1856)

The concept is named after **Sir William Rowan Hamilton** (1805–1865), the Irish mathematician and Astronomer Royal of Ireland, who in 1856 invented a puzzle called the **"Icosian Game."** The game required the player to find a route through the 20 vertices of a regular dodecahedron (a 12-faced polyhedron) such that every vertex is visited exactly once and the player returns to the starting vertex.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Tourist's Itinerary**
> 
> Imagine you are a tourist visiting **Kerala** with a checklist of **10 must-see destinations** (Kochi, Munnar, Thekkady, Alleppey, Varkala, Kovalam, Thrissur, Guruvayur, Wayanad, Kannur). You want to plan a trip that:
> 
> 1. **Visits every city exactly once** (you hate revisiting the same place twice).
> 2. **Returns to your home airport** (Kochi) at the end.
> 3. Uses **existing direct roads/flights** between consecutive cities.
> 
> The sequence of cities satisfying all three conditions is your **Hamiltonian Circuit** of the Kerala travel graph!

If you do not need to return to the start, just visit every city once, then it is a **Hamiltonian Path**.

## 1.4 Eulerian vs Hamiltonian — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Eulerian Path/Circuit | Hamiltonian Path/Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| **What it traverses** | Every **EDGE** exactly once | Every **VERTEX** exactly once |
| **Discovery** | Euler, 1736 (Königsberg bridges) | Hamilton, 1856 (Icosian Game) |
| **Characterization** | **Fully solved** (Theorem-based) | **No general characterization** (NP-complete) |
| **Decision Problem** | Polynomial-time decidable | NP-complete in general |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse "Eulerian" (edges) with "Hamiltonian" (vertices). A single mnemonic: **"H is for Head-count (vertices)"** — Hamiltonian counts **V**ertices.

## 1.5 The Fundamental Difficulty

Unlike Eulerian graphs, where Euler's Theorem provides a **complete and elegant** characterization (a graph is Eulerian iff every vertex has even degree and the graph is connected), no such clean necessary-and-sufficient condition exists for Hamiltonian graphs. We only have:

- **Necessary conditions** (e.g., the Bondy–Chvátal closure operations)
- **Sufficient conditions** (e.g., **Dirac's Theorem, Ore's Theorem**)

This is why Hamiltonian cycle detection is one of the most celebrated **NP-complete** problems in computer science, foundational to the **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** in operations research.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessary Conditions for Hamiltonian Circuits

For a graph $G$ with $n \geq 3$ vertices to have a Hamiltonian circuit, it must satisfy these baseline conditions:

1. **Connectivity:** $G$ must be **connected** (a Hamiltonian path alone requires connectedness; the circuit requires 2-connectedness in the general case).
2. **No vertex of degree 1:** If $\deg(v) = 1$, then $v$ cannot be an internal vertex of any Hamiltonian path; the only way to include $v$ is as an endpoint.
3. **No cut vertex** that isolates vertices: A cut vertex in a graph with a Hamiltonian path can only be an endpoint.
4. **Vertex count vs edge count:** A Hamiltonian graph with $n$ vertices must have at least $n$ edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no degree-1 vertex?** If $\deg(v) = 1$, then $v$ has only one neighbor, say $u$. In any Hamiltonian path visiting $v$, $v$ must use the single edge $(u, v)$. If the path is closed (a circuit), $v$ would need *two* distinct incident edges — but it has only one. **Contradiction!** So $v$ can only be an endpoint of an open Hamiltonian path.

## 2.2 Dirac's Theorem (1952) — A Famous Sufficient Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dirac's Theorem:** Let $G$ be a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices. If
> $$\deg(v) \geq \frac{n}{2} \quad \text{for every vertex } v \in V(G)$$
> then $G$ is **Hamiltonian** (i.e., $G$ contains a Hamiltonian circuit).

**Intuition:** Dirac's condition says the graph is "dense enough" — every vertex touches at least half the other vertices. This density is so strong that it *forces* the graph to contain a Hamiltonian cycle.

## 2.3 Ore's Theorem (1960) — A Generalization of Dirac's

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ore's Theorem:** Let $G$ be a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices. If
> $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n \quad \text{for every pair of non-adjacent vertices } u, v \in V(G)$$
> then $G$ is **Hamiltonian**.

**Key Difference from Dirac:** Dirac requires *every individual vertex* to have degree $\geq n/2$. Ore only requires the *sum of degrees* of non-adjacent pairs to be $\geq n$. Hence Ore's condition is **weaker** (covers more graphs).

**Relationship:** Dirac's Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Ore's Theorem (you can prove Dirac as a corollary of Ore).

## 2.4 Complete Bipartite Graph $K_{m,n}$ and Hamiltonicity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** The complete bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$ (with $m \leq n$) contains a Hamiltonian circuit **if and only if $m = n$** (i.e., $K_{m,m}$).

**Why?** A Hamiltonian circuit must alternate between the two partite sets. For the circuit to close, the two parts must have **equal cardinality**. If $m \neq n$, the circuit cannot return to its starting side.

For Hamiltonian **paths**, $K_{m,n}$ always has one, provided both $m, n \geq 1$.

## 2.5 Bondy–Chvátal Closure Theorem (Conceptual)

Define the **closure** $\text{cl}(G)$ of a graph $G$ as follows: repeatedly add edges between non-adjacent vertices $u, v$ where $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$ until no such pair exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bondy–Chvátal Theorem:** A graph $G$ is Hamiltonian **if and only if** its closure $\text{cl}(G)$ is Hamiltonian.

**Utility:** This gives an *algorithmic* way to test Hamiltonicity for many dense graphs by saturating the degree sum condition.

## 2.6 The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) Connection

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — finding the *minimum-weight* Hamiltonian circuit in a weighted complete graph — is a cornerstone of:
> - **Logistics** (delivery route optimization for Amazon, FedEx, Flipkart)
> - **VLSI chip design** (minimizing wire length on circuit boards)
> - **DNA sequencing** (genome assembly algorithms)
> - **Robotics & autonomous vehicles** (path planning)
> 
> The decision version of TSP is **NP-complete** (Karp's 21 problems, 1972).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Theorem / Concept | Condition / Formula | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dirac's Theorem** | $\deg(v) \geq n/2 \quad \forall v \in V$ | **Sufficient** | Requires $n \geq 3$ |
| 2 | **Ore's Theorem** | $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n \quad \forall u \not\sim v$ | **Sufficient** | Generalizes Dirac |
| 3 | **Hamiltonian in $K_{m,n}$** | Circuit exists iff $m = n$ | **Necessary & Sufficient** | Path exists iff $m, n \geq 1$ |
| 4 | **Petersen Graph** | 3-regular, 10 vertices | **Non-Hamiltonian** | Famous counter-example |
| 5 | **Lower bound on edges** | $e(G) \geq n$ | **Necessary** | For Hamiltonian $G$ |
| 6 | **Bondy–Chvátal** | $G$ Hamiltonian $\iff \text{cl}(G)$ Hamiltonian | **Equivalence** | Useful for proofs |
| 7 | **Pósa's Theorem** | Degree sequence non-decreasing, $\deg(v_k) \geq k$ for $k < n/2$, etc. | **Sufficient** | Generalization of Dirac/Ore |
| 8 | **Tournament** | Every tournament has a Hamiltonian **path** | **Existence theorem** | Rédei's theorem (1934) |
| 9 | **Necessary condition** | No vertex of degree $< 2$ in Hamiltonian circuit | **Necessary** | Endpoint of a path can have $\deg = 1$ |
| 10 | **Complete graph $K_n$** | Has $(n-1)!/2$ distinct Hamiltonian circuits | **Counting** | For $n \geq 3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall Warning:** None of Dirac/Ore are **necessary** conditions. A graph can fail both Dirac and Ore conditions and **still be Hamiltonian**. These theorems only give **sufficient** conditions.

## 2.8 Two Famous Counter-Examples to Memorize

### 2.8.1 The Petersen Graph
The **Petersen graph** (1898, discovered by Julius Petersen) is:
- 3-regular
- Has 10 vertices and 15 edges
- **Non-Hamiltonian** (has no Hamiltonian circuit)
- Has a Hamiltonian **path**

This is the canonical counter-example to many "are these conditions sufficient?" thought experiments.

### 2.8.2 The Hypohamiltonian Graph
A graph is **hypohamiltonian** if it has no Hamiltonian circuit, but removing *any* single vertex produces a Hamiltonian graph. The **Petersen graph is NOT hypohamiltonian**, but the **Coxeter graph** and **Tutte graph** are famous examples.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of Dirac's Theorem (Sketch)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem Statement:** If $G$ is a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices and $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ for all $v$, then $G$ has a Hamiltonian circuit.

**Proof by Contradiction — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 (Setup):** Assume $G$ satisfies Dirac's condition but has **no Hamiltonian circuit**. Since $G$ is connected (otherwise some vertex would have degree $< n/2$), we can extend to a maximal non-Hamiltonian path.

**Step 2 (Maximal Path):** Let $P = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k)$ be a longest path in $G$ that is **not** contained in any Hamiltonian circuit. All neighbors of $v_1$ and $v_k$ must lie **on $P$** (otherwise $P$ could be extended, contradicting maximality).

**Step 3 (Endpoint neighbors):** So $v_1$ is adjacent to some vertices on $P$ — say to $v_{i_1}, v_{i_2}, \ldots, v_{i_d}$ where $d = \deg(v_1) \geq n/2$.

**Step 4 (Endpoint $v_k$):** Similarly, $v_k$ is adjacent to vertices on $P$ — say to $v_{j_1}, \ldots, v_{j_e}$ where $e = \deg(v_k) \geq n/2$.

**Step 5 (Counting argument):** The set of "blocked" positions is $S = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_d\} \cup \{j_1+1, j_2+1, \ldots, j_e+1\}$. We claim $|S| > k-1$, which is a contradiction because all of $S \subseteq \{2, 3, \ldots, k\}$.

Let us make this rigorous:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|S| &= d + e \\
    &= \deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) \\
    &\geq \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{2} \\
    &= n \quad (\text{by Dirac's condition})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 (Derive contradiction):** If $|S| > k-1$, then since $S \subseteq \{2, 3, \ldots, k\}$ and $|\{2, 3, \ldots, k\}| = k-1$, we get $n \leq k$. But $P$ is a maximal path, so $k = n$ (all vertices are on $P$).

**Step 7 (Close the cycle):** There must exist some index $i$ such that $v_1 \sim v_i$ and $v_{i-1} \sim v_k$. Then the cycle $C = v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{i-1}, v_k, v_{k-1}, \ldots, v_i, v_1$ is a Hamiltonian circuit — contradicting our assumption. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Proof That $K_{m,n}$ is Hamiltonian iff $m = n$

**Proof ($\Rightarrow$ direction):** Suppose $K_{m,n}$ with $m \leq n$ has a Hamiltonian circuit $C$. The two partite sets are $A = \{a_1, \ldots, a_m\}$ and $B = \{b_1, \ldots, b_n\}$. Edges in $K_{m,n}$ go only between $A$ and $B$.

**Step 1:** $C$ is a closed walk visiting all $m + n$ vertices.

**Step 2:** In $C$, vertices alternate between $A$ and $B$ (since no edge lies inside $A$ or inside $B$).

**Step 3:** Since $C$ is closed and has length $m + n$, the number of vertices from $A$ visited equals the number from $B$ visited (alternation requires this for closure).

**Step 4:** Therefore $m = n$. $\blacksquare$

**Proof ($\Leftarrow$ direction):** If $m = n$, list $A = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m)$ and $B = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m)$. The walk
$$C: a_1 \to b_1 \to a_2 \to b_2 \to a_3 \to b_3 \to \cdots \to a_m \to b_m \to a_1$$
is a Hamiltonian circuit (every consecutive pair is an edge in $K_{m,n}$). $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Counting Hamiltonian Circuits in $K_n$

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** The number of distinct Hamiltonian circuits in the complete graph $K_n$ (for $n \geq 3$) is
> $$\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Fix a starting vertex $v_1$ (we will divide by $n$ at the end because the circuit is cyclic).

**Step 2:** Visit the remaining $n-1$ vertices in any order: $(n-1)!$ permutations.

**Step 3:** Each Hamiltonian circuit is counted **twice** (clockwise and counter-clockwise traversal give the same cycle).

**Step 4:** So the total is $\dfrac{(n-1)!}{2}$.

**Example:** $K_4$ has $\frac{3!}{2} = 3$ distinct Hamiltonian circuits.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Applying Ore's Theorem

**Problem:** Determine whether the graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edge set
$$E = \{\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{1,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{2,5\}, \{3,4\}, \{3,5\}, \{4,5\}\}$$
is Hamiltonian. Apply Ore's theorem.

**Step 1 (Compute degrees):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(1) &= 3 \quad (\text{neighbors: } 2, 3, 4) \\
\deg(2) &= 3 \quad (\text{neighbors: } 1, 3, 5) \\
\deg(3) &= 4 \quad (\text{neighbors: } 1, 2, 4, 5) \\
\deg(4) &= 3 \quad (\text{neighbors: } 1, 3, 5) \\
\deg(5) &= 3 \quad (\text{neighbors: } 2, 3, 4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (Find non-adjacent pairs and check Ore's condition):**

The only non-adjacent pair is $\{1, 5\}$ (not listed in $E$).

$$
\deg(1) + \deg(5) = 3 + 3 = 6 \geq n = 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 (Conclusion):** Ore's condition is satisfied. By **Ore's Theorem**, $G$ is **Hamiltonian**.

**Step 4 (Verify with explicit circuit):** $1 \to 2 \to 5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ is a valid Hamiltonian circuit of length 5. ✓

## 3.5 Worked Example — Verifying a Non-Hamiltonian Graph

**Problem:** Show that the **Petersen graph** is non-Hamiltonian.

**Step 1 (Setup):** The Petersen graph $P$ has $n = 10$ vertices, is 3-regular, and is vertex-transitive. It has girth 5.

**Step 2 (Argument):** Suppose $P$ has a Hamiltonian cycle $C$ of length 10. The Petersen graph contains 5-cycles (pentagons) and the complement $\overline{P}$ has a specific structure.

**Step 3 (Final):** A rigorous proof uses the fact that $P$ has 6 distinct 5-cycles through any vertex, and a Hamiltonian cycle would partition the 10 vertices into 2 vertices (the ones "between" the 5-cycles) — but the structure of $P$ forbids this. The graph admits no Hamiltonian cycle.

**Conclusion:** The Petersen graph is **non-Hamiltonian** (but has a Hamiltonian path).

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix

| When you see this... | Apply this theorem | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Every $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ | **Dirac's Theorem** | Hamiltonian ✓ |
| Every non-adjacent pair has $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$ | **Ore's Theorem** | Hamiltonian ✓ |
| Bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$ with $m = n$ | Direct construction | Hamiltonian ✓ |
| Bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$ with $m \neq n$ | No cycle possible | **Not Hamiltonian** |
| Graph with a vertex of degree 1 | Trivial obstruction | **Not Hamiltonian** |
| Petersen graph (3-regular, 10 vertices) | Known counter-example | **Not Hamiltonian** |
| Tournament (complete directed graph) | Rédei's theorem | Hamiltonian **path** ✓ |

    A2 -.->|HC: 2| A5

**Legend:** The red dashed edges trace the Hamiltonian circuit $1 \to 2 \to 5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$.

## 4.2 Diagram: Petersen Graph — Non-Hamiltonian Counter-Example

The Petersen graph is a 3-regular graph on 10 vertices. It is the canonical example of a **non-Hamiltonian** graph.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OUTER[Outer Pentagon]
        P1((1)) --- P2((2))
        P2 --- P3((3))
        P3 --- P4((4))
        P4 --- P5((5))
        P5 --- P1
    end
    subgraph INNER[Inner Star]
        Q1((6)) --- Q2((7))
        Q2 --- Q3((8))
        Q3 --- Q4((9))
        Q4 --- Q5((10))
        Q5 --- Q1
    end
    P1 --- Q1
    P2 --- Q2
    P3 --- Q3
    P4 --- Q4
    P5 --- Q5
    classDef petersen stroke:#0000ff,stroke-width:2px;
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5 petersen;
```

**Observation:** Despite being 3-regular (which one might naively think is "dense enough"), the Petersen graph admits **no Hamiltonian circuit**. This shows that Dirac and Ore are *sufficient* but not *necessary* — the Petersen graph is sparse but still has a Hamiltonian *path* (just not a circuit).

## 4.3 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hamiltonian Cycle Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G with n vertices] --> B{Is G connected?}
    B -->|No| C[Output: NO Hamiltonian Circuit]
    B -->|Yes| D{Any vertex with deg < 1?}
    D -->|Yes| C
    D -->|No| E{Any vertex with deg 1?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Output: Only Hamiltonian Path Possible]
    E -->|No| G{Check Dirac: deg v ≥ n/2 for all v?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Output: HAMILTONIAN by Dirac]
    G -->|No| I{Check Ore: deg u + deg v ≥ n for all non-adjacent u,v?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Output: HAMILTONIAN by Ore]
    I -->|No| K{Is G bipartite Km,n with m ≠ n?}
    K -->|Yes| C
    K -->|No| L[Apply Bondy-Chvatal Closure or Backtracking]
    L --> M[Final Decision]
    classDef success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef failure fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px;
    class H,J success;
    class C,F failure;
    class A,B,D,E,G,I,K,L,M process;
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Comparison — Eulerian vs Hamiltonian Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given Graph G] --> B{What do you want to traverse?}
    B -->|Every EDGE once| C[Eulerian Path Circuit]
    B -->|Every VERTEX once| D[Hamiltonian Path Circuit]
    C --> E{Check vertex degrees}
    E -->|All even or exactly 2 odd| F[YES, EULERIAN]
    E -->|Otherwise| G[NO]
    D --> H{Check Dirac Ore Bondy-Chvatal}
    H -->|Sufficient condition met| I[YES, HAMILTONIAN]
    H -->|Not met| J{Exhaustive search DP n factorial}
    J -->|Cycle found| I
    J -->|No cycle| G2[NO]
    classDef yes fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef no fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px;
    class F,I yes;
    class G,G2 no;
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Hamiltonian circuit. Explain with a small example why Dirac's theorem provides only a sufficient (and not necessary) condition for Hamiltonicity.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hamiltonian circuit** in a simple graph $G$ is a closed walk that visits every vertex of $G$ exactly once and returns to the starting vertex. If $G$ has $n$ vertices, the circuit has exactly $n$ edges.

**Why Dirac's condition is sufficient but not necessary:**

- **Sufficient:** Dirac's theorem guarantees that *if* $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ for all $v$, *then* the graph is Hamiltonian. It is a one-way implication.
- **Not necessary:** There exist Hamiltonian graphs that violate Dirac's condition.

**Example:** Consider a cycle graph $C_5$ on 5 vertices. Each vertex has $\deg(v) = 2 < 5/2 = 2.5$, so Dirac's condition fails. Yet $C_5$ **is** Hamiltonian (it is itself a Hamiltonian cycle). This proves Dirac is **not necessary**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Ore's theorem. How does it relate to Dirac's theorem?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Ore's Theorem:** If $G$ is a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices and for every pair of non-adjacent vertices $u, v \in V(G)$,
> $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$$
> then $G$ contains a Hamiltonian circuit.

**Relationship to Dirac's Theorem:**

Dirac's theorem is a **special case** of Ore's theorem. To see this, suppose $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ for all $v \in V$. For any two non-adjacent vertices $u, v$:
$$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{2} = n$$
This is exactly Ore's condition. Hence every graph satisfying Dirac's condition also satisfies Ore's, so Dirac's theorem is a **corollary** of Ore's theorem. Ore's theorem covers a strictly **larger** class of graphs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**. Either OR choice applies between (a) and (b). Solve the chosen path completely.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** State and prove **Dirac's theorem** on Hamiltonian circuits. Apply it to verify Hamiltonicity of a graph on 6 vertices where every vertex has degree at least 3.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the graph $G$ with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and edges 
$$E = \{AB, AC, AD, AE, BC, BD, BE, BF, CD, CE, DF, EF\}$$ 
(i) Compute the degree of each vertex. (ii) Check if Ore's condition is satisfied. (iii) If yes, find an explicit Hamiltonian circuit.

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Dirac's Theorem Proof**

**Statement:** Let $G$ be a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices. If $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ for every vertex $v$, then $G$ has a Hamiltonian circuit.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Since every vertex has degree $\geq n/2 \geq 1$, $G$ is connected. If $G$ is not Hamiltonian, we can find a longest path $P = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k)$ that cannot be extended into a Hamiltonian circuit. All neighbors of $v_1$ and $v_k$ lie on $P$.

**Step 2:** Define two index sets:

$$A = \{i : v_1 \sim v_{i+1}, \ 2 \leq i+1 \leq k\}$$

$$B = \{i : v_k \sim v_i, \ 2 \leq i \leq k\}$$

Note $|A| = \deg(v_1)$ and $|B| = \deg(v_k)$.

**Step 3:** We claim $A \cap B = \emptyset$. If there exists $i \in A \cap B$, then $v_1 \sim v_{i+1}$ and $v_k \sim v_i$. This gives the cycle
$$v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to v_i \to v_k \to v_{k-1} \to \cdots \to v_{i+1} \to v_1$$
which is a Hamiltonian cycle — **contradiction**.

**Step 4:** Since $A, B \subseteq \{2, 3, \ldots, k\}$ with $A \cap B = \emptyset$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|A \cup B| &\leq k - 1 \\
|A| + |B| &\leq k - 1 \\
\deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) &\leq k - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** But by Dirac's condition, $\deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) \geq n/2 + n/2 = n$. So $n \leq k - 1$, i.e., $k \geq n + 1$. But the path has at most $n$ vertices, so $k = n$ (path is maximal).

**Step 6:** Combining $k = n$ with Step 4, we get $\deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) \leq n - 1 < n$ — a contradiction with Dirac's condition.

**Conclusion:** $G$ must have a Hamiltonian circuit. $\blacksquare$

**Application:** A graph on $n = 6$ vertices with every $\deg(v) \geq 3 = 6/2$ satisfies Dirac's condition, so it is Hamiltonian.

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution for the Given Graph**

**(i) Degree computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(A) &= 4 \quad (B, C, D, E) \\
\deg(B) &= 5 \quad (A, C, D, E, F) \\
\deg(C) &= 4 \quad (A, B, D, E) \\
\deg(D) &= 4 \quad (A, B, C, F) \\
\deg(E) &= 4 \quad (A, B, C, F) \\
\deg(F) &= 3 \quad (B, D, E)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Ore's condition check:** Find all non-adjacent pairs and verify $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq 6$.

Non-adjacent pairs: $\{A, F\}$, $\{C, F\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(A) + \deg(F) &= 4 + 3 = 7 \geq 6 \quad \checkmark \\
\deg(C) + \deg(F) &= 4 + 3 = 7 \geq 6 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ore's condition is satisfied for all non-adjacent pairs.** Therefore, by Ore's theorem, $G$ is Hamiltonian.

**(iii) Explicit Hamiltonian circuit:**

$$A \to C \to E \to F \to D \to B \to A$$

**Verification:** Each consecutive pair is an edge in $E$? 
- $A$–$C$ ✓
- $C$–$E$ ✓
- $E$–$F$ ✓
- $F$–$D$ ✓
- $D$–$B$ ✓
- $B$–$A$ ✓ (closing the cycle)

All 6 vertices visited exactly once. ✓

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) [7 marks]** State and prove that the complete bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$ (with $m \leq n$) contains a Hamiltonian circuit **if and only if** $m = n$. Give a clear construction for the $K_{m,m}$ case.

**(b) [7 marks]** The **Petersen graph** $P$ is a 3-regular graph on 10 vertices. (i) Show that $P$ has no Hamiltonian circuit. (ii) Does $P$ have a Hamiltonian **path**? Justify.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Hamiltonicity of $K_{m,n}$**

**Theorem:** $K_{m,n}$ with $m \leq n$ has a Hamiltonian circuit $\iff m = n$.

**Proof ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $K_{m,n}$ has a Hamiltonian circuit $C$ with partite sets $A = \{a_1, \ldots, a_m\}$ and $B = \{b_1, \ldots, b_n\}$. Since edges in $K_{m,n}$ only join $A$ to $B$, the circuit must alternate between $A$ and $B$. As the circuit is closed, the number of $A$-vertices equals the number of $B$-vertices visited. Hence $m = n$.

**Proof ($\Leftarrow$):** Suppose $m = n$. Label $A = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m)$ and $B = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m)$. Construct the walk:
$$C: a_1 \to b_1 \to a_2 \to b_2 \to a_3 \to b_3 \to \cdots \to a_m \to b_m \to a_1$$

Every consecutive pair $(a_i, b_i)$ and $(b_i, a_{i+1})$ is an edge of $K_{m,m}$ by completeness. $C$ visits all $2m$ vertices and returns to $a_1$. So $C$ is a Hamiltonian circuit.

**Example:** $K_{3,3}$ has Hamiltonian circuit $a_1 \to b_1 \to a_2 \to b_2 \to a_3 \to b_3 \to a_1$. ✓

---

#### **Part (b) — Petersen Graph Analysis**

**(i) No Hamiltonian circuit in $P$:**

The Petersen graph has 10 vertices, 15 edges, is 3-regular, vertex-transitive, and has girth 5. A Hamiltonian circuit $C$ of length 10 in $P$ would mean $P$ can be decomposed as a 10-cycle plus 5 additional "chord" edges. 

By a parity/structural argument: $P$ has 6 distinct 5-cycles through any given vertex. If $C$ is a Hamiltonian cycle, the 5 vertices not adjacent to $v$ on $C$ would have to form a "matching" into the Petersen structure — but the Petersen graph's complement $\overline{P}$ is the **Kneser graph** $KG(5,2)$ (the Petersen graph is the Kneser graph itself), and the structure is incompatible. Detailed proof requires checking that $P$ has no spanning cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **For exam purposes:** State that the Petersen graph is a well-known 3-regular non-Hamiltonian graph discovered in 1898, and cite that exhaustive verification shows no Hamiltonian cycle exists. Full proof requires case analysis or use of Tutte's theorem (1956).

**(ii) Hamiltonian path exists:** 

Yes, $P$ does have a Hamiltonian path. One such path is:
$$v_1 \to v_2 \to v_3 \to v_4 \to v_5 \to v_6 \to v_7 \to v_8 \to v_9 \to v_{10}$$
where the vertices are labeled according to the standard Petersen diagram. This is a well-established result.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Examiner's Common Pitfall Warnings (Read Carefully to Avoid Losing Marks):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Sufficient" with "Necessary":** Students often write "Ore's condition is satisfied, so the graph is NOT Hamiltonian" — completely wrong direction! The theorem is *sufficient*, not *necessary*. **(−2 marks deduction common)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $n \geq 3$:** Dirac and Ore require $n \geq 3$. Applying them to a 2-vertex graph is meaningless. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Mixing Eulerian with Hamiltonian:** A common blunder is to check "all even degrees" for a Hamiltonian question. Euler = edges, Hamiltonian = vertices! **(−2 to −3 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Counting the Hamiltonian circuit length:** Always state explicitly that a Hamiltonian circuit in a graph of $n$ vertices has length exactly $n$ and visits each vertex **exactly once** (not just "at least once").
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Bondy–Chvátal closure equivalence:** When asked to "show that $G$ is Hamiltonian," you may strengthen $G$ by adding edges (without changing Hamiltonicity) until Dirac's or Ore's condition is met, and then apply the theorem. This is a powerful examiner-pleasing technique.
> 
> 6. **Not verifying the circuit:** When asked to "find a Hamiltonian circuit," never forget to **close the cycle** (return to start) and to confirm every edge used is in $E$. **(−2 marks)**
> 
> 7. **Confusing $K_{m,n}$ paths with circuits:** $K_{2,3}$ has a Hamiltonian **path** but **no Hamiltonian circuit** because $2 \neq 3$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Hamiltonian Paths and Circuits**
> 
> - [ ] **Hamiltonian Path:** Visits every **vertex** of $G$ exactly once (open walk).
> - [ ] **Hamiltonian Circuit:** Closes the path back to the start vertex; has length $n$ in an $n$-vertex graph.
> - [ ] **Eulerian vs Hamiltonian:** Euler = edges, Hamiltonian = vertices (mnemonic: **"H = Head-count"**).
> - [ ] **Dirac's Theorem:** $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ for all $v$ $\Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian. (**Sufficient**, $n \geq 3$).
> - [ ] **Ore's Theorem:** $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$ for all non-adjacent $u, v$ $\Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian. (**Generalizes** Dirac).
> - [ ] **$K_{m,n}$ Hamiltonicity:** Circuit exists $\iff m = n$. Path exists $\iff m, n \geq 1$.
> - [ ] **Petersen Graph:** 3-regular, 10 vertices, **non-Hamiltonian** (canonical counter-example).
> - [ ] **Bondy–Chvátal Closure:** $G$ is Hamiltonian $\iff \text{cl}(G)$ is Hamiltonian (algorithmic test).
> - [ ] **Counting:** $K_n$ has $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$ distinct Hamiltonian circuits.
> - [ ] **Tournament (Rédei, 1934):** Every tournament has a Hamiltonian **path**.
> - [ ] **TSP connection:** Min-weight Hamiltonian circuit in a weighted complete graph is **NP-hard**.
> - [ ] **Necessary conditions are weak:** No degree-1 vertex, $e(G) \geq n$, $G$ connected.
> - [ ] **Dirac/Ore are sufficient but NOT necessary:** $C_5$ is Hamiltonian but violates Dirac ($\deg = 2 < 2.5$).
> - [ ] **NP-Completeness:** The Hamiltonian cycle decision problem is one of Karp's 21 NP-complete problems.
> - [ ] **Engineering Applications:** Logistics, VLSI routing, DNA sequencing, robotics, network design.
> - [ ] **Always verify** by listing edges of an explicit Hamiltonian circuit when answering "find" questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Travelling Salesman Problem - Directed Graphs - Types of Digraphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Travelling Salesman Problem - Directed Graphs - Types of Digraphs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > The Travelling Salesman Problem is a classic combinatorial optimization problem. Given a weighted complete graph $G = (V, E, w)$ representing $n$ cities and the cost $w(u, v)$ of travelling between every pair of distinct cities $u, v \in V$, the goal is to find a **Hamiltonian cycle** (a closed tour) of **minimum total weight** that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city. * **Search Space Cardinality:** For $n$ cities, the number of distinct TSP tours is exactly $\dfrac{(n-1)!}{2}$, since we fix one city as the start, arrange the remaining $(n-1)$ in any order, and then divide by **2** because reversing any tour gives the same closed loop. * **Decision vs Optimization:** The decision version (Does a tour of cost $\leq k$ exist?) is **NP-Complete**, while the optimization version is **NP-Hard**. #### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a delivery executive in Kochi who must drop parcels at 7 different locations across Kerala and return to the warehouse. The GPS gives a distance matrix. The exec wants the **shortest possible loop** that touches every location exactly once. Brute-force trying all $7!/2 = 2520$ routes is feasible; but for 50 cities, $49!/2 \approx 3 \times 10^{62}$ routes makes brute-force impossible — this explosion of possibilities is the heart of TSP. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** TSP is *not* solvable in polynomial time for arbitrary $n$ (unless $P = NP$). All known exact algorithms are exponential. Hence, the focus in KTU is on **formulation**, **brute-force enumeration**, and the **Held–Karp dynamic programming** lower bound. --- ### 1.2 Directed Graphs (Digraphs) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A **directed graph** or **digraph** is an ordered pair $D = (V, A)$, where $V$ is a non-empty set of **vertices** and $A \subseteq V \times V$ is a set of **arcs** (or **directed edges**). Each arc is an ordered pair $(u, v)$ indicating direction from $u$ (the **tail**) to $v$ (the **head**). * **Ordered Pair:** Unlike undirected edges $\{u, v\}$, the arc $(u, v)$ is **not** the same as $(v, u)$. * **Loops:** An arc of the form $(v, v)$ is a **loop**. * **Multiple Arcs:** Two or more arcs with the same tail and head are **parallel arcs** (multi-digraph). #### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy Think of a one-way street map of a city. The street from "Junction A" to "Junction B" is an arc $(A, B)$, but you cannot legally travel from B to A on that street. A digraph captures such asymmetry — for instance, one-way traffic, water flowing through pipes, or web-page hyperlinks. --- ### 1.3 Fundamental Degree Definitions in Digraphs For any vertex $v \in V$: * **In-degree** $d^{-}(v)$: number of arcs whose **head** is $v$. * **Out-degree** $d^{+}(v)$: number of arcs whose **tail** is $v$. * **Total degree** $d(v) = d^{-}(v) + d^{+}(v)$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Identity (First Theorem of Digraph Theory):** > $$\sum_{v \in V} d^{-}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} d^{+}(v) = \vert A \vert$$ > *This is because every arc contributes exactly **1** to one in-degree and exactly **1** to one out-degree.* --- ### 1.4 Types of Digraphs — Quick Snapshot > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies digraphs based on **arc structure**, **degree regularity**, and **reachability**. Master the **9 standard types** below. | # | Type | Defining Property | |---|------|-------------------| | 1 | Simple Digraph | No loops, no parallel arcs | | 2 | Complete (Symmetric) Digraph | Both $(u,v)$ and $(v,u)$ exist for every distinct pair | | 3 | Asymmetric Digraph | If $(u,v) \in A$, then $(v,u) \notin A$ | | 4 | Symmetric Digraph | $(u,v) \in A \implies (v,u) \in A$ | | 5 | Balanced Digraph | $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for all $v$ | | 6 | Regular Digraph | $d^{+}(v) = k$ (constant) for all $v$ | | 7 | Tournament | Exactly one of $(u,v)$ or $(v,u)$ for every pair | | 8 | Transitive Digraph | $(u,v), (v,w) \in A \implies (u,w) \in A$ | | 9 | DAG | No directed cycle | | 10 | Strongly Connected | Path from $u$ to $v$ for all $u, v$ | | 11 | Weakly Connected | Underlying undirected graph is connected | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 TSP — Structural Decomposition * **Tour (Hamiltonian Cycle) in a digraph:** A sequence of $n$ distinct vertices $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$ such that for every $i$, the arc $(v_i, v_{i+1})$ exists, plus the closing arc $(v_n, v_1)$ exists. * **Symmetric TSP (STSP):** Cost matrix is symmetric, i.e., $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$. Number of tours = $(n-1)!/2$. * **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP):** Cost matrix is *not* symmetric, i.e., $c_{ij} \neq c_{ji}$ in general. Number of tours = $(n-1)!$ (no symmetric collapse). #### 2.1.1 Why is TSP Important? * **Engineering Utility:** VLSI chip drilling, circuit-board wiring, DNA sequencing, vehicle routing, robot motion planning, network packet scheduling. * **Graph-Theoretic Bridge:** TSP connects *Hamiltonian cycles*, *permutations*, and *integer linear programming* into one elegant structure. --- ### 2.2 Solving TSP — The KTU-Aligned Algorithmic Hierarchy > [!NOTE] > KTU Module 2 expects you to know the **brute-force method**, the **Nearest-Neighbour heuristic**, and the **Held–Karp DP lower bound**. #### A. Brute-Force Exact Method * Enumerate **all** $(n-1)!/2$ (STSP) or $(n-1)!$ (ATSP) tours. * Compute weight of each; pick minimum. * Time Complexity: $O(n!)$ — **only feasible for $n \leq 12$**. #### B. Nearest-Neighbour Heuristic (Greedy) 1. Start at any vertex $v_1$. 2. From current vertex, go to the **unvisited** vertex with minimum arc weight. 3. Repeat step 2 until all vertices visited. 4. Return to $v_1$. * Time Complexity: $O(n^2)$. * **Caveat:** Does *not* guarantee optimality. Classic counter-example: 4 cities with weights 1, 2, 3, 10, 20, 25. #### C. Held–Karp Dynamic Programming (Optimal Exact) * Define $f(S, i)$ = minimum cost of a path that starts at a fixed vertex $s$, visits every vertex in subset $S$, and ends at vertex $i$. * Recurrence: $$f(S, i) = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} \left[ f(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + c_{ji} \right]$$ * Base case: $f(\{i\}, i) = c_{s,i}$. * Final answer: $\displaystyle \min_{i} \left[ f(V \setminus \{s\}, i) + c_{i, s} \right]$. * **Time Complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$. **Space Complexity:** $O(n \cdot 2^n)$. * For $n = 20$, this is $\approx 4 \times 10^8$ ops — feasible; for $n = 50$ it becomes $\approx 10^{17}$ — infeasible. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Identity | Units / Cardinality | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Number of STSP tours | $\dfrac{(n-1)!}{2}$ | Pure number | $n \geq 3$ | | Number of ATSP tours | $(n-1)!$ | Pure number | No reversal symmetry | | Brute-force complexity | $O(n!)$ | Operations | Infeasible for $n > 12$ | | Held–Karp complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | Operations | Optimal exact | | Nearest-Neighbour complexity | $O(n^2)$ | Operations | Heuristic, sub-optimal | | Sum of in-degrees | $\sum d^{-}(v) = \vert A \vert$ | Arcs | First Theorem | | Sum of out-degrees | $\sum d^{+}(v) = \vert A \vert$ | Arcs | First Theorem | | Max arcs in simple digraph | $n(n-1)$ | Arcs | No loops, no parallel | | Max arcs in complete digraph | $n(n-1)$ | Arcs | Symmetric pair counts as 2 | | Max arcs in tournament | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Arcs | Exactly one per pair | | Balanced condition | $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for all $v$ | — | Euler-tour friendly | | Strongly connected size | $\geq n$ | Vertices | Plus reachable paths | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** In the **complete digraph**, between any two vertices $u, v$ there are **two** arcs $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$, so the arc count is $n(n-1)$, not $\binom{n}{2}$. Students often confuse this with the tournament count. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why TSP / Digraphs? | |---|---| | **VLSI Chip Drilling** | Minimize drill-head travel across thousands of holes | | **Postal / Courier Delivery** | Route optimization across cities | | **Network Packet Routing** | Shortest Hamiltonian path in directed weighted graphs | | **Logistics (Amazon, FedEx)** | Capacitated vehicle routing generalizes TSP | | **Telecommunication Arcs** | One-way fiber links are naturally digraphs | | **Compiler DAGs** | Instruction scheduling, dependency resolution | | **Citation Networks** | Direction = "cites" — inherently digraph | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — TSP on 4 Cities (Brute-Force) Consider 4 cities with the **symmetric** cost matrix $C$ (upper triangle shown; $C$ is symmetric, diagonal is $\infty$): $$ C = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & \infty & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & \infty & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & \infty \end{pmatrix} $$ We fix city **1** as the start/end. There are $(4-1)!/2 = 3$ unique tours. #### Tour 1: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ $$ W_1 = c_{12} + c_{23} + c_{34} + c_{41} = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95 $$ *Explanation:* We traverse $1 \to 2$ (cost 10), then $2 \to 3$ (cost 35), then $3 \to 4$ (cost 30), then return $4 \to 1$ (cost 20). The total weight is the sum of the four arc costs. #### Tour 2: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ $$ W_2 = c_{12} + c_{24} + c_{43} + c_{31} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80 $$ *Explanation:* We travel $1 \to 2$ (cost 10), then $2 \to 4$ (cost 25), then $4 \to 3$ (cost 30), then $3 \to 1$ (cost 15). Total = 80. #### Tour 3: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1$ $$ W_3 = c_{13} + c_{32} + c_{24} + c_{41} = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95 $$ *Explanation:* The third tour is $1 \to 3$ (15), $3 \to 2$ (35), $2 \to 4$ (25), and $4 \to 1$ (20). Sum = 95. > [!NOTE] > **Optimal TSP Tour:** $\boxed{1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1 \text{ with cost } 80}$ (minimum among 95, 80, 95). --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Nearest-Neighbour Heuristic on the Same Graph Starting at city **1**: * **Step 1:** From city 1, smallest unvisited arc is $c_{12} = 10$. Go to 2. * **Step 2:** From city 2 (visited = {1, 2}), unvisited = {3, 4}. Minimum outgoing arc: $c_{24} = 25$. Go to 4. * **Step 3:** From city 4 (visited = {1, 2, 4}), unvisited = {3}. Forced: $c_{43} = 30$. Go to 3. * **Step 4:** Return $3 \to 1$: $c_{31} = 15$. $$ \text{NN Tour Cost} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80 $$ *Explanation:* Coincidentally, the nearest-neighbour heuristic gives the optimal 80 here. But this is not always true — the heuristic has no optimality guarantee. --- ### 3.3 Held–Karp DP Step-by-Step on the 4-City TSP Fix source $s = 1$. Define $f(S, i)$ = min cost from 1 visiting set $S$ and ending at $i$. **Stage 1 — Base cases** (subsets of size 1, $S = \{i\}$): $$ f(\{2\}, 2) = c_{12} = 10 $$ $$ f(\{3\}, 3) = c_{13} = 15 $$ $$ f(\{4\}, 4) = c_{14} = 20 $$ **Stage 2 — Subsets of size 2:** $$ f(\{2,3\}, 2) = \min\Big[ f(\{3\}, 3) + c_{32} \Big] = 15 + 35 = 50 $$ $$ f(\{2,3\}, 3) = \min\Big[ f(\{2\}, 2) + c_{23} \Big] = 10 + 35 = 45 $$ $$ f(\{2,4\}, 2) = f(\{4\}, 4) + c_{42} = 20 + 25 = 45 $$ $$ f(\{2,4\}, 4) = f(\{2\}, 2) + c_{24} = 10 + 25 = 35 $$ $$ f(\{3,4\}, 3) = f(\{4\}, 4) + c_{43} = 20 + 30 = 50 $$ $$ f(\{3,4\}, 4) = f(\{3\}, 3) + c_{34} = 15 + 30 = 45 $$ **Stage 3 — Subsets of size 3:** $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) = \min\Big[ f(\{3,4\},3) + c_{32},\; f(\{3,4\},4) + c_{42} \Big] = \min[50+35,\; 45+25] = 70 $$ $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) = \min\Big[ f(\{2,4\},2) + c_{23},\; f(\{2,4\},4) + c_{43} \Big] = \min[45+35,\; 35+30] = 65 $$ $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) = \min\Big[ f(\{2,3\},2) + c_{24},\; f(\{2,3\},3) + c_{34} \Big] = \min[50+25,\; 45+30] = 75 $$ **Stage 4 — Close the tour back to source 1:** $$ \text{OPT} = \min_{i \in \{2,3,4\}} \left[ f(\{2,3,4\}, i) + c_{i1} \right] $$ $$ \text{OPT} = \min\Big[ 70 + c_{21},\; 65 + c_{31},\; 75 + c_{41} \Big] = \min[70+10,\; 65+15,\; 75+20] = 80 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Held–Karp confirms $\boxed{OPT = 80}$, matching the brute-force. Trace back: $f(\{2,3,4\},3) = 65$ came from $f(\{2,4\},4) = 35$ via $c_{43}=30$; then $f(\{2,4\},4) = 35$ came from $f(\{2\},2) = 10$ via $c_{24}=25$; then $c_{12} = 10$ and $c_{31} = 15$. Reconstructed tour: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$, cost 80. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Digraph-Type Decision Procedure Given a digraph $D = (V, A)$ and a candidate property, apply this **decision flow**: | Step | Check | Property Concluded | |------|-------|--------------------| | 1 | No $(v, v) \in A$ and no parallel arcs? | Simple digraph | | 2 | For all $u \neq v$: both $(u,v), (v,u) \in A$? | Complete (symmetric) digraph | | 3 | For all $u \neq v$: exactly one of $(u,v), (v,u) \in A$? | Tournament | | 4 | $(u,v) \in A \iff (v,u) \in A$? | Symmetric digraph | | 5 | $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for all $v$? | Balanced digraph | | 6 | $d^{+}(v) = k$ for all $v$, for some constant $k$? | $k$-regular out-digraph | | 7 | $(u,v), (v,w) \in A \implies (u,w) \in A$? | Transitive digraph | | 8 | No directed cycle exists? | DAG (acyclic digraph) | | 9 | For all $u, v$: path from $u$ to $v$ and $v$ to $u$? | Strongly connected | | 10 | Underlying undirected graph is connected, but 9 fails? | Weakly connected (not strong) | > [!NOTE] > **Example Application:** A directed 4-cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ is a **balanced** digraph (each $d^- = d^+ = 1$), a **1-regular** out-digraph, and a **strong** digraph. But it is *not* simple only if loops exist — and it is *not* a tournament (no arc between non-adjacent pairs). --- ### 3.5 Derivation: Why $\sum d^{-}(v) = \sum d^{+}(v) = |A|$ Let $D = (V, A)$ be any digraph. For each arc $a = (u, v) \in A$: * $a$ contributes exactly **1** to $d^{+}(u)$ (since $u$ is its tail). * $a$ contributes exactly **1** to $d^{-}(v)$ (since $v$ is its head). Therefore, summing over all arcs: $$ \sum_{v \in V} d^{+}(v) = \sum_{a \in A} 1 = \vert A \vert $$ $$ \sum_{v \in V} d^{-}(v) = \sum_{a \in A} 1 = \vert A \vert $$ *Explanation:* Each arc is counted exactly once in the out-degree summation (by its tail) and exactly once in the in-degree summation (by its head). Since both summations enumerate every arc, the totals must equal $|A|$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consequence:** In a *balanced* digraph, $\sum d^{-}(v) = \sum d^{+}(v)$ gives $n \cdot d = \vert A \vert$ when all vertices have the same degree $d$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Optimal TSP Tour Visualization (4-City Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1((City 1
START)) node2((City 2)) node3((City 3)) node4((City 4)) node1 -- "10" --> node2 node2 -- "25" --> node4 node4 -- "30" --> node3 node3 -- "15" --> node1 style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style node3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style node4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` * **Reading the diagram:** The arrows trace the optimal Hamiltonian cycle $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ with arc weights 10, 25, 30, 15, totalling **80**. The yellow city is the start/end point; the colors differentiate the four cities. --- ### 4.2 Tournament on 4 Vertices ```mermaid flowchart TD v1((A)) v2((B)) v3((C)) v4((D)) v1 -- "A beats B" --> v2 v1 -- "A beats C" --> v3 v4 -- "D beats A" --> v1 v2 -- "B beats C" --> v3 v3 -- "C beats D" --> v4 v2 -- "B beats D" --> v2 style v1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000 style v2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B style v3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style v4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B ``` * **Reading the diagram:** Between every pair of vertices, *exactly one* directed arc exists. This is the definition of a **tournament**. The above is the *transitive tournament* $A \to B \to C \to D$ plus $A \to C$ and $A \to D$ and $B \to D$, which is a total order. --- ### 4.3 DAG vs. Cyclic Digraph — Side-by-Side ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DAG["DAG No directed cycle"] a1((X)) --> b1((Y)) a1 --> c1((Z)) b1 --> d1((W)) c1 --> d1 end subgraph CYC["Cyclic Digraph Strong component"] a2((P)) --> b2((Q)) b2 --> c2((R)) c2 --> a2 end style a1 fill:#90EE90 style b1 fill:#90EE90 style c1 fill:#90EE90 style d1 fill:#90EE90 style a2 fill:#FFB6C1 style b2 fill:#FFB6C1 style c2 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` * **Reading the diagram:** The left subgraph (DAG) has no way to return to a starting vertex, so it is **acyclic**. The right subgraph forms a 3-cycle $P \to Q \to R \to P$, so it is *not* a DAG. The DAG is the foundation of topological sorting and partial orders. --- ### 4.4 Strongly Connected vs Weakly Connected Digraph ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph STRONG["Strongly Connected Every vertex reachable from every other"] s1((1)) --> s2((2)) s2 --> s3((3)) s3 --> s4((4)) s4 --> s1 s1 --> s3 end subgraph WEAK["Weakly Connected Underlying undirected graph is connected, but not strong"] w1((5)) --> w2((6)) w3((7)) --> w1 w2 --> w4((8)) end style s1 fill:#FFD700 style s2 fill:#FFD700 style s3 fill:#FFD700 style s4 fill:#FFD700 style w1 fill:#D3D3D3 style w2 fill:#D3D3D3 style w3 fill:#D3D3D3 style w4 fill:#D3D3D3 ``` * **Reading the diagram:** In the **strongly connected** left component, the cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ plus the chord $1 \to 3$ guarantees reachability in both directions. In the **weakly connected** right component, although ignoring arc directions makes a connected tree, there is *no* path from vertex 7 to vertex 8 (both are sinks/isolated in the directed sense). --- ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TSP Solution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[Input n cities and Cost Matrix C] --> VAL[Validate C symmetric and diagonal infinite] VAL --> CHK{n small leq 12} CHK -- Yes --> BF[Brute Force Enumerate n minus 1 factorial over 2 tours] CHK -- No --> DP[Held Karp Dynamic Programming O of n squared times 2 to the n] BF --> COMP[Compute tour weights] DP --> COMP COMP --> MIN[Select minimum weight tour] MIN --> OUT[Output Optimal Hamiltonian Cycle and Cost] style IN fill:#E6E6FA style BF fill:#FFE4B5 style DP fill:#FFE4B5 style OUT fill:#98FB98 ``` * **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline begins with **input validation** of the cost matrix $C$ (KTU expects symmetric/diagonal-$\infty$ assumptions). For small $n$, the system uses **brute-force enumeration**; for larger $n$, it switches to the **Held–Karp DP** which is still exponential but exponentially faster. Both routes feed into the **min-selection block** and emit the optimal tour. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Q1. Define the Travelling Salesman Problem. For a 5-city symmetric TSP, how many distinct tours are possible? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) is a combinatorial optimization problem in which, given a weighted complete graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with $n$ cities, one must find a Hamiltonian cycle of minimum total weight that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city. > > For $n = 5$ cities, the number of distinct tours is: > $$\frac{(n-1)!}{2} = \frac{4!}{2} = \frac{24}{2} = 12 \text{ tours}$$ > > Each tour is a closed loop; we fix one city as origin, permute the remaining $(n-1) = 4$ cities, and divide by $2$ because reversing the traversal yields the same cycle. --- **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **Q2. Define a Tournament. Show that a tournament on 3 vertices always contains a directed Hamiltonian cycle. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > A **tournament** is a digraph in which every pair of distinct vertices is joined by exactly one arc (i.e., for every pair $\{u, v\}$, exactly one of $(u, v)$ or $(v, u)$ is in $A$). > > **Claim:** Every tournament on $n \geq 3$ vertices contains a Hamiltonian cycle. > > **Proof for $n = 3$:** Let the three vertices be $a, b, c$. The three arcs form one of two configurations: > 1. A directed 3-cycle $a \to b \to c \to a$ — this *is* a Hamiltonian cycle. > 2. A transitive triple $a \to b, a \to c, b \to c$ — wait, this has no cycle! Counter-check: but by **Redei's Theorem** (1934), every tournament has a Hamiltonian path, and a tournament on 3 vertices that is not a 3-cycle is a *transitive* tournament, which is acyclic. > > **Correction:** A tournament on 3 vertices is *not always* a Hamiltonian cycle; it is a cycle if and only if it is **strong** (i.e., $a \to b, b \to c, c \to a$). A transitive tournament $a \to b, a \to c, b \to c$ has no directed cycle. (Redei's theorem guarantees a *Hamiltonian path*, not necessarily a cycle, for arbitrary tournaments; strong tournaments contain cycles.) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B requires an **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]** **Q.A(a)** Consider the following weighted complete digraph with 4 vertices. The cost matrix $C$ (with $c_{ii} = \infty$) is given below. Using **Held–Karp dynamic programming**, find the **optimal TSP tour** and its minimum cost. **(7 Marks)** $$ C = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 8 & 12 & 6 \\ 8 & \infty & 10 & 9 \\ 12 & 10 & \infty & 7 \\ 6 & 9 & 7 & \infty \end{pmatrix} $$ **Model Solution:** Fix source $s = 1$. Define $f(S, i)$ as min cost from 1, visiting set $S$, ending at $i$. **Stage 1 — Singletons:** $$ f(\{2\}, 2) = 8, \quad f(\{3\}, 3) = 12, \quad f(\{4\}, 4) = 6 $$ **Stage 2 — Pairs:** $$ f(\{2,3\}, 2) = f(\{3\}, 3) + c_{32} = 12 + 10 = 22 $$ $$ f(\{2,3\}, 3) = f(\{2\}, 2) + c_{23} = 8 + 10 = 18 $$ $$ f(\{2,4\}, 2) = f(\{4\}, 4) + c_{42} = 6 + 9 = 15 $$ $$ f(\{2,4\}, 4) = f(\{2\}, 2) + c_{24} = 8 + 9 = 17 $$ $$ f(\{3,4\}, 3) = f(\{4\}, 4) + c_{43} = 6 + 7 = 13 $$ $$ f(\{3,4\}, 4) = f(\{3\}, 3) + c_{34} = 12 + 7 = 19 $$ **Stage 3 — Triples:** $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) = \min\big[f(\{3,4\},3) + c_{32},\; f(\{3,4\},4) + c_{42}\big] = \min[13+10,\; 19+9] = 23 $$ $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) = \min\big[f(\{2,4\},2) + c_{23},\; f(\{2,4\},4) + c_{43}\big] = \min[15+10,\; 17+7] = 24 $$ $$ f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) = \min\big[f(\{2,3\},2) + c_{24},\; f(\{2,3\},3) + c_{34}\big] = \min[22+9,\; 18+7] = 25 $$ **Stage 4 — Close the tour:** $$ \text{OPT} = \min\big[f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) + c_{21},\; f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) + c_{31},\; f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) + c_{41}\big] $$ $$ = \min[23+8,\; 24+12,\; 25+6] = \min[31,\; 36,\; 31] = 31 $$ **Traceback:** The minimum 31 is achieved by ending at vertex 2 (cost 23) or vertex 4 (cost 25). Choosing $f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) = 25$ via $f(\{2,3\}, 3) = 18$ via $f(\{2\}, 2) = 8$: > Reconstructed tour: $\boxed{1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1}$ with cost $8 + 10 + 7 + 6 = 31$. **Q.A(b)** Define a **Strongly Connected Digraph** and a **Weakly Connected Digraph**. Determine whether the following digraph $D$ is strongly connected, weakly connected, or both: $$ V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad A = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (1,4), (4,2)\} $$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Strongly Connected Digraph:** A digraph in which, for every ordered pair of vertices $u, v$, there exists a *directed* path from $u$ to $v$ and from $v$ to $u$. > > **Weakly Connected Digraph:** A digraph in which the *underlying undirected graph* (replace every arc by an undirected edge) is connected. **Reachability Analysis for $D$:** * From 1: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 4 \to 2 \dots$ — can reach 1, 2, 3, 4. * From 2: $2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 4 \to 2 \dots$ — can reach 1, 2, 3, 4. * From 3: $3 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \dots$ and $1 \to 4$, so can reach 1, 2, 3, 4. * From 4: $4 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 4$ — can reach 1, 2, 3, 4. > **Conclusion:** $D$ is **Strongly Connected** (and hence also weakly connected). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]** **Q.B(a)** Define the following with one example each: **(i) Balanced Digraph (ii) Tournament (iii) Transitive Digraph (iv) DAG** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** > **(i) Balanced Digraph:** A digraph in which the in-degree equals the out-degree at *every* vertex, i.e., $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for all $v \in V$. > > *Example:* A directed 4-cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$. Each vertex has $d^{-} = d^{+} = 1$. ✓ Balanced. > **(ii) Tournament:** A digraph in which, for every pair of distinct vertices $u, v$, *exactly one* of the arcs $(u, v)$ or $(v, u)$ is present. > > *Example:* On 3 vertices, $1 \to 2, 2 \to 3, 1 \to 3$. This is a transitive tournament; alternatively $1 \to 2, 2 \to 3, 3 \to 1$ is a cyclic tournament. > **(iii) Transitive Digraph:** A digraph in which, for all $u, v, w \in V$, if $(u, v) \in A$ and $(v, w) \in A$, then $(u, w) \in A$. > > *Example:* $1 \to 2, 2 \to 3, 1 \to 3$ — this is transitive because $1 \to 2$ and $2 \to 3$ together imply $1 \to 3$ (which exists). > **(iv) DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph):** A digraph with no directed cycle. > > *Example:* $1 \to 2, 1 \to 3, 2 \to 4, 3 \to 4$. There is no way to return to any starting vertex via directed arcs. --- **Q.B(b)** Prove that in **any digraph** $D = (V, A)$: $$ \sum_{v \in V} d^{-}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} d^{+}(v) = \vert A \vert $$ Use this to show that in a *balanced* digraph with 6 vertices each of degree 4, the total number of arcs is $12$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Proof:** Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\}$ enumerate all arcs of $D$. For each arc $a_i = (u_i, v_i)$: > > * $a_i$ contributes exactly **one unit** to $d^{+}(u_i)$ (its tail). > * $a_i$ contributes exactly **one unit** to $d^{-}(v_i)$ (its head). > > Therefore, summing over all arcs: > $$\sum_{v \in V} d^{+}(v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} 1 = m = \vert A \vert$$ > $$\sum_{v \in V} d^{-}(v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} 1 = m = \vert A \vert$$ > > Hence $\sum d^{-}(v) = \sum d^{+}(v) = \vert A \vert$. $\blacksquare$ > **Application:** In a balanced digraph, $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for every $v$. If every vertex has $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v) = 2$ (since "degree 4" means $d^{-} + d^{+} = 4$ and they are equal, so each is 2): > $$\vert A \vert = \sum_{v \in V} d^{+}(v) = 6 \times 2 = 12 \text{ arcs.}$$ > > *Verification:* $\sum d^{-}(v) = 6 \times 2 = 12$ also, confirming the identity. --- ### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in TSP and Digraph Questions:** > 1. **Tour count:** Forgetting the division by $2$ in symmetric TSP — students write $(n-1)!$ instead of $(n-1)!/2$. **Cost: up to 1 mark.** > 2. **Held–Karp indexing:** Mixing up $S$ (visited set) with $i$ (ending vertex) — the recurrence $f(S, i) = \min_{j}[f(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + c_{ji}]$ is *not* symmetric. **Cost: up to 3 marks.** > 3. **Tournament vs. Complete Digraph:** A complete digraph has *two* arcs per pair; a tournament has *one*. Confusing these gives wrong arc counts. **Cost: up to 2 marks.** > 4. **Strong vs. Weak Connectivity:** A strongly connected digraph is *also* weakly connected, but the converse fails. Saying "weakly connected $\implies$ strong" loses marks. **Cost: up to 2 marks.** > 5. **DAG vs. Tournament:** A tournament on $n \geq 3$ vertices *can* contain a cycle — it is *not* a DAG in general. **Cost: up to 1 mark.** > 6. **Diagonal entries:** Cost matrix diagonal must be $\infty$ (or a sentinel like $-1$ for *unreachable*) — never $0$. **Cost: up to 1 mark.** --- ### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** * ✅ **TSP Definition:** Find min-weight Hamiltonian cycle in weighted complete graph. NP-Hard optimization, NP-Complete decision. * ✅ **Tour Count:** Symmetric $= (n-1)!/2$, Asymmetric $= (n-1)!$. * ✅ **Exact Methods:** Brute-force $O(n!)$ for $n \leq 12$; Held–Karp DP $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ for $n \leq 25$. * ✅ **Heuristic:** Nearest-Neighbour $O(n^2)$ — fast but *not* optimal. * ✅ **Digraph:** Ordered pair $(u, v)$ — direction matters. Sum of in-degrees = sum of out-degrees = $|A|$. * ✅ **Simple Digraph:** No loops, no parallel arcs. * ✅ **Complete Digraph:** Both $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$ for every pair $\Rightarrow$ arc count $n(n-1)$. * ✅ **Tournament:** Exactly one of $(u, v)$ or $(v, u)$ for every pair $\Rightarrow$ arc count $\binom{n}{2}$. * ✅ **Balanced Digraph:** $d^{-}(v) = d^{+}(v)$ for all $v$. * ✅ **Regular Digraph:** All vertices have the same out-degree $k$ (or in-degree). * ✅ **Transitive Digraph:** $(u, v), (v, w) \in A \implies (u, w) \in A$. * ✅ **DAG:** No directed cycle — basis of topological sort, partial orders, critical path. * ✅ **Strongly Connected:** Path $u \to v$ and $v \to u$ for *all* pairs. **Implies** weakly connected. * ✅ **Weakly Connected:** Underlying undirected graph is connected. **Does not imply** strong. * ✅ **Strong Component:** Maximal strongly connected sub-digraph inside a larger digraph. * ✅ **Eulerian Connection:** A *balanced + strongly connected* digraph has an Euler tour (every arc traversed exactly once). * ✅ **TSP-Digraph Link:** A TSP tour is a Hamiltonian cycle in a complete *symmetric* digraph; ATSP uses a complete *asymmetric* digraph.

topics.title: Digraphs and Binary Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digraphs and Binary Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Digraphs and Binary Relations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Directed Graph (Digraph)

A **directed graph** (or **digraph**) is an ordered pair $D = (V, A)$, where:

- $V$ is a finite, non-empty set of objects called **vertices** (or **nodes**).
- $A \subseteq V \times V$ is a set of ordered pairs $(u, v)$ with $u, v \in V$, called **arcs** (or **directed edges**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** In a digraph, every arc $(u, v)$ is *ordered*; the arc $(u, v)$ is distinct from $(v, u)$. The vertex $u$ is the **tail** (start) and $v$ is the **head** (end). Loops of the form $(u, u)$ are usually permitted only when explicitly stated.

**Formal notation:**
$$D = (V, A), \quad A = \{(u, v) \mid u, v \in V\}$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "One-Way Roads"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **city map** where every road is one-way. Some roads allow you to drive from A to B, but not from B back to A. The intersections are the **vertices**, and the one-way streets are the **arcs**. The direction matters! A digraph is exactly this — a network where each connection has a definite direction of travel.

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Vertex $v$ | A city intersection |
| Arc $(u, v)$ | A one-way street from $u$ to $v$ |
| Out-degree of $v$ | Number of streets leaving $v$ |
| In-degree of $v$ | Number of streets entering $v$ |
| Loop $(v, v)$ | A roundabout returning to itself |

### 1.3 Formal Definition — Binary Relation

A **binary relation** $R$ from a set $A$ to a set $B$ is a subset of the Cartesian product:
$$R \subseteq A \times B = \{(a, b) \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$$

If $A = B$, then $R$ is called a **relation on $A$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A binary relation on a finite set $A$ can always be represented as a digraph on $V = A$, where $(a, b) \in R$ corresponds to the arc $a \to b$. This **digraph representation of relations** is a core Module 2 outcome.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Family Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the relation **"is the parent of"** on a set of people. If Alice is the parent of Bob, we write $(\text{Alice}, \text{Bob}) \in R$. This is *not symmetric* — Bob is not the parent of Alice. This directed, asymmetric nature is exactly why we use digraphs to visualise such relations.

### 1.5 Degree Metrics

For any vertex $v$ in a digraph $D = (V, A)$:

$$\text{Out-degree: } d^{+}(v) = \vert \{u \in V \mid (v, u) \in A\}\vert$$

$$\text{In-degree: } d^{-}(v) = \vert \{u \in V \mid (u, v) \in A\}\vert$$

$$\text{Total degree: } d(v) = d^{+}(v) + d^{-}(v)$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Logic — Why Digraphs Model Binary Relations

Every binary relation $R$ on a finite set $V$ has a **one-to-one correspondence** with a digraph $D = (V, A)$ where:
$$(u, v) \in R \iff \text{arc } u \to v \in A$$

This correspondence means all **graph-theoretic** operations (paths, reachability, connectivity) translate directly to **relation-theoretic** operations (composition, powers, closure). This is the foundational bridge between Module 2 and Module 3 (reachability, transitive closure).

### 2.2 Properties of Binary Relations (KTU High-Yield)

Let $R$ be a relation on a set $A$. The following table consolidates all KTU-exam-relevant properties:

| Property | Definition (Formal) | In Digraph Terms |
|---|---|---|
| **Reflexive** | $\forall x \in A,\ (x, x) \in R$ | Every vertex has a loop |
