# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define a *Minimum Spanning Tree* of a connected weighted graph. State any **two differences** between Prim's algorithm and Kruskal's algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

A *Minimum Spanning Tree* (MST) of a connected weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ is a spanning tree whose total edge weight is the minimum possible among all spanning trees of $G$. That is, $T$ is an MST iff $T$ is a spanning tree and $w(T) \le w(T')$ for every other spanning tree $T'$.

| **Sl.** | **Prim's Algorithm** | **Kruskal's Algorithm** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertex-driven: grows one connected tree from a chosen root. | Edge-driven: builds a forest that eventually merges into a tree. |
| 2 | At each step, picks the *minimum-weight edge crossing the current cut*. | At each step, picks the *minimum-weight edge that does not form a cycle*. |
| 3 | Time complexity: $O(\mid V \mid^{2})$ (matrix) or $O(\mid E \mid \log \mid V \mid)$ (heap). | Time complexity: $O(\mid E \mid \log \mid E \mid)$. |
| 4 | Preferred for **dense** graphs. | Preferred for **sparse** graphs. |

**(2 marks for the MST definition + 1 mark for valid differences.)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** What is the **Cycle Property** of MSTs? Use it to justify the rejection of a heavy edge in Kruskal's algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

**Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in the graph $G$, the edge of **maximum weight** on $C$ is **never** part of any MST of $G$.

**Justification for Kruskal's algorithm:**
Consider an edge $e = (u, v)$ that Kruskal's algorithm rejects because adding it would close a cycle $C$. At the moment of inspection, $u$ and $v$ are already connected through the rest of the cycle, and every other edge on $C$ has weight $\le w(e)$ (otherwise Kruskal would have inspected them later). Thus $e$ is the **maximum-weight edge on $C$**, so by the Cycle Property, $e$ **cannot** belong to any MST. Hence, discarding $e$ is safe and the algorithm remains correct.

**(2 marks for statement + 1 mark for justification.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation Note:** *"Candidates must answer Part B questions with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Internal choice is permitted between two full question stems."*

---

### 🔹 Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]`

> **Q (B-A):** Consider the following weighted undirected graph $G$ with $\mid V \mid = 6$ and $\mid E \mid = 9$:
>
> | **Edge** | **Weight** |
> |---|---|
> | (1, 2) | 4 |
> | (1, 3) | 2 |
> | (2, 3) | 1 |
> | (2, 4) | 5 |
> | (3, 5) | 8 |
> | (4, 5) | 6 |
> | (4, 6) | 3 |
> | (5, 6) | 7 |
> | (3, 4) | 9 |
>
> **(a)** Find the MST using **Prim's algorithm** starting from vertex **1**. Show every step and compute the total weight. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the MST of the same graph using **Kruskal's algorithm**. Show all intermediate steps, including any edge that is rejected, and verify the total weight matches Part (a). **(7 Marks)**

#### 🔸 Model Solution for B-A (a) — Prim's Algorithm

We maintain $S$ (visited set) and $T$ (edge set of MST).

**Iteration 1:**
$$
S = \{1\},\quad \text{Frontier} = \{(1,2,4),\ (1,3,2)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(1, 3, 2)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 3\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2)\}$.

**Iteration 2:**
$$
\text{Frontier} = \{(1, 2, 4),\ (2, 3, 1),\ (3, 5, 8),\ (3, 4, 9)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(2, 3, 1)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2),\ (2, 3, 1)\}$.

**Iteration 3:**
$$
\text{Frontier (effective)} = \{(1, 2, 4) \text{ drop, both in } S \text{ now } \Rightarrow \text{ already in } S \text{, keep},\ (2, 4, 5),\ (3, 5, 8),\ (3, 4, 9)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(1, 2, 4)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2),\ (2, 3, 1),\ (1, 2, 4)\}$.

**Iteration 4:**
$$
S = \{1, 2, 3\},\quad \text{Frontier} = \{(2, 4, 5),\ (3, 5, 8),\ (3, 4, 9)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(2, 4, 5)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2),\ (2, 3, 1),\ (1, 2, 4),\ (2, 4, 5)\}$.

**Iteration 5:**
$$
S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\},\quad \text{Frontier (effective)} = \{(3, 5, 8),\ (4, 5, 6),\ (4, 6, 3)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(4, 6, 3)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2),\ (2, 3, 1),\ (1, 2, 4),\ (2, 4, 5),\ (4, 6, 3)\}$.

**Iteration 6:**
$$
S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6\},\quad \text{Frontier (effective)} = \{(3, 5, 8),\ (4, 5, 6),\ (5, 6, 7)\}
$$
Minimum edge: $(4, 5, 6)$. Add it. $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$, $T = \{(1, 3, 2),\ (2, 3, 1),\ (1, 2, 4),\ (2, 4, 5),\ (4, 6, 3),\ (4, 5, 6)\}$.

$$
\mid T \mid = 6 - 1 = 5 = \mid V \mid - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{STOP.}
$$

$$
\boxed{w(T_{\text{Prim}}) = 2 + 1 + 4 + 5 + 3 + 6 = \mathbf{21}}
$$

#### 🔸 Model Solution for B-A (b) — Kruskal's Algorithm

**Step 0 — Sort Edges Ascending:**

$$
(2,3,1) < (1,3,2) < (4,6,3) < (1,2,4) < (2,4,5) < (4,5,6) < (5,6,7) < (3,5,8) < (3,4,9)
$$

| **Step** | **Edge** | **Weight** | **Find(u) vs Find(v)** | **Action** | **Components After** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (2, 3) | 1 | $\{2\} \neq \{3\}$ | **ADD** | $\{2,3\},\ \{1\},\ \{4\},\ \{5\},\ \{6\}$ |
| 2 | (1, 3) | 2 | $\{1\} \neq \{2,3\}$ | **ADD** | $\{1,2,3\},\ \{4\},\ \{5\},\ \{6\}$ |
| 3 | (4, 6) | 3 | $\{4\} \neq \{6\}$ | **ADD** | $\{1,2,3\},\ \{4,6\},\ \{5\}$ |
| 4 | (1, 2) | 4 | $\{1,2,3\} = \{1,2,3\}$ | **REJECT** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 5 | (2, 4) | 5 | $\{1,2,3\} \neq \{4,6\}$ | **ADD** | $\{1,2,3,4,6\},\ \{5\}$ |
| 6 | (4, 5) | 6 | $\{1,2,3,4,6\} \neq \{5\}$ | **ADD** | $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ |

$\mid T \mid = 5 = \mid V \mid - 1 \Rightarrow$ **STOP.** (Edges (5,6,7), (3,5,8), (3,4,9) are **not processed**.)

$$
\boxed{w(T_{\text{Kruskal}}) = 1 + 2 + 3 + 5 + 6 = \mathbf{17}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistake:**
> In Part (a) of B-A, students often forget to **drop edges from the frontier** when both endpoints are already in $S$. They then accidentally add an edge like $(1, 2, 4)$ *after* $(2, 3, 1)$, producing a cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$ and **losing 4–5 marks**.
>
> **Mitigation:** After every edge addition, **immediately** mark both endpoints as visited and *prune* the frontier by removing any edge whose both endpoints are now in $S$.
>
> **For Part (b):** The most common error is forgetting to **explicitly state which edge is rejected and why** (cycle formation). KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for correctly identifying the *rejection step* with the cycle named.

---

### 🔹 Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]` — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **Q (B-B):** **(a)** State and prove the **Cut Property** of MSTs. Show that Prim's algorithm is a direct application of this property. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Apply Kruskal's algorithm to the graph whose edges (with weights) are listed below. Show the Union-Find operations at each step. Compute the MST weight. **(7 Marks)**
>
> | **Edge** | **Weight** |
> |---|---|
> | (a, b) | 4 |
> | (a, d) | 7 |
> | (b, c) | 8 |
> | (b, d) | 11 |
> | (b, e) | 2 |
> | (c, e) | 5 |
> | (c, f) | 6 |
> | (d, e) | 9 |
> | (d, g) | 14 |
> | (e, f) | 10 |
> | (e, g) | 12 |
> | (f, g) | 13 |

#### 🔸 Model Solution for B-B (a) — Cut Property

**Statement (Cut Property):**
Let $G = (V, E, w)$ be a connected weighted graph, and let $(S, V \setminus S)$ be any **cut** of $G$ (i.e., $S \subseteq V$, $S \neq \emptyset$, $S \neq V$). Let $e^{\ast}$ be a **minimum-weight edge crossing the cut** (with ties broken arbitrarily). Then there exists an MST of $G$ that contains $e^{\ast}$.

**Proof:**
Let $T$ be any MST of $G$. If $T$ already contains $e^{\ast}$, we are done. Otherwise, $T$ does not contain $e^{\ast}$. Adding $e^{\ast}$ to $T$ creates a unique **cycle** $C$ in $T \cup \{e^{\ast}\}$. Since $e^{\ast}$ crosses the cut, $C$ must contain at least one other edge $e'$ that also crosses the same cut. By definition, $w(e') \ge w(e^{\ast})$. 

Now form $T' = T \cup \{e^{\ast}\} \setminus \{e'\}$. Clearly:
- $T'$ has the same number of edges as $T$ and remains a spanning tree (we removed an edge from a cycle, restoring acyclicity).
- $w(T') = w(T) - w(e') + w(e^{\ast}) \le w(T)$.

Since $T$ is minimum, $w(T') = w(T)$, so $T'$ is also an MST. Moreover, $T'$ **contains $e^{\ast}$**. This proves the claim. $\blacksquare$

**Application to Prim's Algorithm:**
At every step, Prim's algorithm maintains a set $S$ of vertices already in the tree. It then picks the **minimum-weight edge crossing the cut** $(S, V \setminus S)$ — *exactly* the edge $e^{\ast}$ in the Cut Property. By the Cut Property, this edge belongs to **some** MST. The algorithm repeats this for $\mid V \mid - 1$ iterations, each time adding a guaranteed MST edge, until it has built a complete MST. Hence Prim's algorithm is a direct constructive application of the Cut Property.

#### 🔸 Model Solution for B-B (b) — Kruskal's Algorithm

**Step 0 — Sort Edges:**

$$
(b,e,2) < (a,b,4) < (c,e,5) < (c,f,6) < (a,d,7) < (b,c,8) < (d,e,9) < (e,f,10) < (b,d,11) < (e,g,12) < (f,g,13) < (d,g,14)
$$

$\mid V \mid = 7 \Rightarrow$ we need $\mid T \mid = 6$ edges. Initial components: $\{a\}, \{b\}, \{c\}, \{d\}, \{e\}, \{f\}, \{g\}$.

| **Step** | **Edge** | **Weight** | **Find(u) vs Find(v)** | **Action** | **Components After** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (b, e) | 2 | $\{b\} \neq \{e\}$ | **ADD** | $\{b,e\},\ \{a\},\ \{c\},\ \{d\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\}$ |
| 2 | (a, b) | 4 | $\{a\} \neq \{b,e\}$ | **ADD** | $\{a,b,e\},\ \{c\},\ \{d\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\}$ |
| 3 | (c, e) | 5 | $\{c\} \neq \{a,b,e\}$ | **ADD** | $\{a,b,c,e\},\ \{d\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\}$ |
| 4 | (c, f) | 6 | $\{a,b,c,e\} \neq \{f\}$ | **ADD** | $\{a,b,c,e,f\},\ \{d\},\ \{g\}$ |
| 5 | (a, d) | 7 | $\{a,b,c,e,f\} \neq \{d\}$ | **ADD** | $\{a,b,c,d,e,f\},\ \{g\}$ |
| 6 | (b, c) | 8 | Same! | **REJECT** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 7 | (d, e) | 9 | Same! | **REJECT** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 8 | (e, f) | 10 | Same! | **REJECT** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 9 | (b, d) | 11 | Same! | **REJECT** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 10 | (e, g) | 12 | $\{a,b,c,d,e,f\} \neq \{g\}$ | **ADD** | $\{a,b,c,d,e,f,g\}$ |

$\mid T \mid = 6 = \mid V \mid - 1 \Rightarrow$ **STOP.** (Edges (f,g,13) and (d,g,14) are not processed.)

**MST Edges and Weight:**

$$
T = \{(b,e,2),\ (a,b,4),\ (c,e,5),\ (c,f,6),\ (a,d,7),\ (e,g,12)\}
$$

$$
\boxed{w(T_{\text{Kruskal}}) = 2 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 + 12 = \mathbf{36}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — B-B (b) Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show the FIND step explicitly** — Examiners allocate 1 mark for clearly showing the component lookup using Union-Find. Just writing "no cycle, add" is **not enough**; you must write something like *"Find($b$) = $\{b,e\}$, Find($e$) = $\{b,e\}$ ⇒ same component ⇒ reject"*.
> 2. **Skipping edges in the table** — Even rejected edges must be shown in the trace; otherwise the examiner cannot verify your cycle-check reasoning. Skipping them may cost **2–3 marks** in valuation.
> 3. **Wrong termination** — Some students stop after 5 edges (e.g., when they first connect all components through (a,d,7)) and miss (e,g,12). Always verify: *the algorithm stops only when $\mid T \mid = \mid V \mid - 1$*, **not** when the graph first "appears connected."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **MST definition** = a spanning tree of **minimum total weight**; always has exactly $\mid V \mid - 1$ edges.
- [x] **Spanning tree** = connected + acyclic + spans all vertices.
- [x] **Prim's algorithm** = **vertex-driven**, grows one tree, uses the **Cut Property**, complexity $O(\mid V \mid^{2})$ or $O(\mid E \mid \log \mid V \mid)$ with binary heap.
- [x] **Kruskal's algorithm** = **edge-driven**, merges components, uses the **Cycle Property**, complexity $O(\mid E \mid \log \mid E \mid)$.
- [x] **Cut Property** = minimum-weight edge across any cut is in *some* MST.
- [x] **Cycle Property** = maximum-weight edge on any cycle is in *no* MST.
- [x] **Best fit:** Prim's for **dense** graphs, Kruskal's for **sparse** graphs.
- [x] **Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find)** with path compression and union-by-rank gives nearly $O(1)$ amortized per operation.
- [x] **MST is not unique** when multiple edges share the same minimum weight across a cut.
- [x] **For disconnected graphs**, both algorithms return a **Minimum Spanning Forest** (one MST per connected component).
- [x] **KTU exam golden rule:** Always end the answer with the line *"$\mid T \mid = \mid V \mid - 1$ edges, total weight = $W$"* — this single line is worth **1–2 marks** in valuation.
- [x] **Real-world applications** to mention in 2-mark theory questions: *VLSI design, telecom networks, road planning, image segmentation, TSP approximation.*
- [x] **Both algorithms are greedy AND provably optimal** for the MST problem on graphs with **distinct** edge weights. (With ties, at least one greedy run still yields an MST.)

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Prim's Algorithm & Kruskal's Algorithm**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm** is a greedy, single-source shortest path algorithm proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1956)** that finds the shortest path from a designated **source vertex** $s$ to every other vertex in a weighted, connected graph $G = (V, E, w)$ where all edge weights are **non-negative** real numbers, i.e., $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for every edge $(u, v) \in E$. **Mathematical Statement:** For a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative weight function $w: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and a source vertex $s \in V$, Dijkstra's algorithm computes the **shortest-path distance** $\delta(s, v)$ for all $v \in V$, where: $$ \delta(s, v) = \min \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(v_{i-1}, v_i) \; : \; \langle s = v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k = v \rangle \text{ is a path from } s \text{ to } v \right\} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: GPS Navigation System** > Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kochi** to **Delhi**. Google Maps must compute the *shortest (or fastest) route*. The cities are **vertices**, the roads connecting them are **edges**, and the road distances/travel times are the **edge weights**. Dijkstra's algorithm acts like a smart navigator that *gradually expands* the confirmed shortest routes city-by-city — once it locks in the shortest path to a nearby city, that decision is **never reversed** (this is the *greedy* nature of the algorithm). **Intuitive Breakdown for a First-Time Learner:** - The algorithm **starts at a chosen source vertex** and assigns it a tentative distance of **0**. - Every other vertex is initially marked as **unvisited** with a tentative distance of **$\infty$** (infinity). - At each step, it picks the **unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance**, marks it as **visited**, and tries to "relax" (improve) the tentative distances of its neighbors. - This process continues until **all reachable vertices have been visited** or the target vertex is finalized. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly states that Dijkstra's algorithm is taught under "Module 3 — Shortest Path Algorithms" and is examined via direct graph problems requiring distance tables and shortest-path tree construction. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Source Vertex | $s$ | The starting vertex from which shortest paths are computed | | Tentative Distance | $d[v]$ | Current best known shortest distance from $s$ to $v$ | | Final Distance | $\delta(s, v)$ | True shortest-path distance from $s$ to $v$ | | Visited Set | $S$ | Set of vertices whose shortest distances are finalized | | Relaxation | — | Operation of updating $d[v]$ via $d[u] + w(u, v)$ | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation — The "Why" and "How" Dijkstra's algorithm is built upon three core principles: 1. **Greedy Choice Property:** At every iteration, the algorithm selects the unvisited vertex with the **minimum** tentative distance. This vertex is then *permanently* added to the visited set $S$, because no other unvisited path can produce a shorter distance to it (this is provable by the triangle inequality since all weights are non-negative). 2. **Edge Relaxation:** For every edge $(u, v)$ emanating from the newly visited vertex $u$, the algorithm checks whether reaching $v$ through $u$ gives a shorter path than the current tentative $d[v]$. The relaxation operation is: $$ \text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \text{ then } d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v) $$ 3. **Optimal Substructure:** Once a vertex $v$ is added to $S$, its shortest distance $\delta(s, v)$ is finalized. This is because any alternative path to $v$ would have to traverse some unvisited vertex with a distance $\geq d[v]$ (by greedy selection), and adding non-negative weights can only increase the total. ## 2.2 Structured Algorithmic Steps **Input:** Weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative weights and source vertex $s$. **Output:** Shortest distance $d[v]$ and predecessor $\pi[v]$ for every vertex $v \in V$. **Step 1 — Initialization:** - For all $v \in V$: set $d[v] = \infty$, $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$ - Set $d[s] = 0$ - Initialize the unvisited set $Q = V$ (typically a min-priority queue) **Step 2 — Main Loop:** While $Q \neq \emptyset$: - Extract the vertex $u \in Q$ with the **minimum** $d[u]$ (i.e., $u = \text{extract-min}(Q)$) - Add $u$ to the visited set $S$ - For every neighbor $v$ of $u$ that is still in $Q$: - Perform relaxation: compute $d[u] + w(u, v)$ - If $d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$, then update $d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v)$ and $\pi[v] = u$ **Step 3 — Termination:** When $Q$ is empty, the algorithm terminates. The array $d[\cdot]$ contains the shortest distances, and the predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$ can be used to reconstruct shortest paths. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the high-yield formula reference table for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|---------|----------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | **Relaxation Operation** | if $d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)$, then $d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$ | Applies only when $v$ is unvisited | | 2 | **Initial Distances** | $d[s] = 0$; $d[v] = \infty$ for $v \neq s$ | $s$ = source vertex | | 3 | **Triangle Inequality** | $\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ | Property of shortest paths | | 4 | **Upper Bound Property** | $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ for all $v$ | Invariant throughout execution | | 5 | **Convergence Property** | When $u$ is extracted, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ | Holds for non-negative weights | | 6 | **Time Complexity (Naïve)** | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Array-based implementation | | 7 | **Time Complexity (Binary Heap)** | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | Min-priority queue implementation | | 8 | **Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap)** | $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Optimal theoretical bound | | 9 | **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | For adjacency list + distance array | | 10 | **Applicability Condition** | $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for all edges | **Negative edges are NOT allowed** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the relaxation condition direction. The update happens only when the **new path is strictly shorter**, i.e., $d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$. Writing $\leq$ or reversing the inequality is a common mark-losing error. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Application of Dijkstra's Algorithm | |--------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Computer Networks** | Routing protocols like **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** and **IS-IS** | | **Transportation & GPS** | Google Maps, Uber, Ola — finding fastest/shortest route | | **VLSI Circuit Design** | Wire-length minimization in chip layout | | **Telecommunications** | Optimal signal routing with minimum latency | | **Game Development (AI)** | Pathfinding for NPC movement in real-time strategy games | | **Operations Research** | Minimum-cost logistics, supply chain optimization | | **Social Networks** | Degrees of separation, shortest connection between users | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Illustrative Worked Example — Complete Exhaustive Trace > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest distance from source vertex **$1$** to all other vertices in the following weighted undirected graph. **Graph Definition:** - Vertices: $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ - Edges and Weights: - $(1, 2)$ with weight $\mathbf{2}$ - $(1, 3)$ with weight $\mathbf{4}$ - $(2, 3)$ with weight $\mathbf{1}$ - $(2, 4)$ with weight $\mathbf{7}$ - $(3, 4)$ with weight $\mathbf{3}$ - $(3, 5)$ with weight $\mathbf{6}$ - $(4, 5)$ with weight $\mathbf{2}$ **Step 1 — Initialization:** We set $d[1] = 0$ and all other tentative distances to $\infty$. The visited set $S = \emptyset$. $$ \begin{aligned} d[1] &= 0, \quad d[2] = \infty, \quad d[3] = \infty, \quad d[4] = \infty, \quad d[5] = \infty \\ \pi[1] &= \text{NIL}, \quad \pi[2] = \pi[3] = \pi[4] = \pi[5] = \text{NIL} \\ S &= \emptyset \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Iteration 1:** Pick the unvisited vertex with minimum $d[\cdot]$, which is vertex $1$ (since $d[1] = 0$). Add $1$ to $S$: $S = \{1\}$. Relax all edges from $1$: - **Edge $(1, 2)$:** Compute $d[1] + w(1, 2) = 0 + 2 = 2$. Since $2 < \infty$, update $d[2] = 2$ and $\pi[2] = 1$. - **Edge $(1, 3)$:** Compute $d[1] + w(1, 3) = 0 + 4 = 4$. Since $4 < \infty$, update $d[3] = 4$ and $\pi[3] = 1$. Updated state: $$ d[\cdot] = [0, \; 2, \; 4, \; \infty, \; \infty], \quad S = \{1\} $$ **Step 3 — Iteration 2:** Among unvisited vertices $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$, the minimum $d[\cdot]$ is $d[2] = 2$. Pick vertex $2$. Add $2$ to $S$: $S = \{1, 2\}$. Relax all edges from $2$ to unvisited neighbors $\{3, 4\}$ (vertex $1$ is already in $S$): - **Edge $(2, 3)$:** Compute $d[2] + w(2, 3) = 2 + 1 = 3$. Since $3 < d[3] = 4$, update $d[3] = 3$ and $\pi[3] = 2$. - **Edge $(2, 4)$:** Compute $d[2] + w(2, 4) = 2 + 7 = 9$. Since $9 < \infty$, update $d[4] = 9$ and $\pi[4] = 2$. Updated state: $$ d[\cdot] = [0, \; 2, \; 3, \; 9, \; \infty], \quad S = \{1, 2\} $$ **Step 4 — Iteration 3:** Among unvisited $\{3, 4, 5\}$, the minimum is $d[3] = 3$. Pick vertex $3$. Add $3$ to $S$: $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$. Relax all edges from $3$ to unvisited neighbors $\{4, 5\}$: - **Edge $(3, 4)$:** Compute $d[3] + w(3, 4) = 3 + 3 = 6$. Since $6 < d[4] = 9$, update $d[4] = 6$ and $\pi[4] = 3$. - **Edge $(3, 5)$:** Compute $d[3] + w(3, 5) = 3 + 6 = 9$. Since $9 < \infty$, update $d[5] = 9$ and $\pi[5] = 3$. Updated state: $$ d[\cdot] = [0, \; 2, \; 3, \; 6, \; 9], \quad S = \{1, 2, 3\} $$ **Step 5 — Iteration 4:** Among unvisited $\{4, 5\}$, the minimum is $d[4] = 6$. Pick vertex $4$. Add $4$ to $S$: $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Relax edges from $4$ to unvisited neighbors $\{5\}$: - **Edge $(4, 5)$:** Compute $d[4] + w(4, 5) = 6 + 2 = 8$. Since $8 < d[5] = 9$, update $d[5] = 8$ and $\pi[5] = 4$. Updated state: $$ d[\cdot] = [0, \; 2, \; 3, \; 6, \; 8], \quad S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\} $$ **Step 6 — Iteration 5:** Only unvisited vertex is $5$ with $d[5] = 8$. Pick vertex $5$. Add $5$ to $S$: $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. No unvisited neighbors remain. Algorithm terminates. **Final Shortest Distances from Source Vertex $1$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(1, 1) &= 0 \\ \delta(1, 2) &= 2 \\ \delta(1, 3) &= 3 \\ \delta(1, 4) &= 6 \\ \delta(1, 5) &= 8 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Master Trace Table (KTU Exam Reference Format) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **exact table format** expected in KTU answer scripts for full marks. | Iteration | Visited Vertex $u$ | $d[1]$ | $d[2]$ | $d[3]$ | $d[4]$ | $d[5]$ | Predecessor Updates | |:---------:|:------------------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:--------------------| | Init | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | — | | 1 | **1** | 0 | **2** | **4** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\pi[2]=1, \pi[3]=1$ | | 2 | **2** | 0 | 2 | **3** | **9** | $\infty$ | $\pi[3]=2, \pi[4]=2$ | | 3 | **3** | 0 | 2 | 3 | **6** | **9** | $\pi[4]=3, \pi[5]=3$ | | 4 | **4** | 0 | 2 | 3 | 6 | **8** | $\pi[5]=4$ | | 5 | **5** | 0 | 2 | 3 | 6 | 8 | — (Final) | ## 3.3 Shortest Path Tree Reconstruction Using the predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$, we trace backwards from each vertex to source $1$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path } 1 \to 2 &: \quad 1 \to 2 \quad (\text{weight } 2) \\ \text{Path } 1 \to 3 &: \quad 1 \to 2 \to 3 \quad (\text{weight } 2 + 1 = 3) \\ \text{Path } 1 \to 4 &: \quad 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \quad (\text{weight } 2 + 1 + 3 = 6) \\ \text{Path } 1 \to 5 &: \quad 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \quad (\text{weight } 2 + 1 + 3 + 2 = 8) \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Correctness (Sketch) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to understand the **greedy correctness** argument, not reproduce it verbatim. **Claim:** When a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. **Proof by Contradiction:** Assume $u$ is the first vertex extracted with $d[u] \neq \delta(s, u)$. Then $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$, meaning there exists a shortest path $P$ from $s$ to $u$. Consider the **first edge** $(x, y)$ on $P$ where $x \in S$ and $y \notin S$ (such an edge must exist because $s \in S$ and $u \notin S$). By the relaxation property, $d[y] = \delta(s, y) \leq \delta(s, u) \leq d[u]$ (by the upper-bound invariant). But this contradicts the fact that $u$ was the *minimum* unvisited vertex extracted. Hence $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. $\blacksquare$ **Key Assumption Used:** All edge weights are **non-negative**, ensuring that once we reach $y$ via $x$, no shorter detour through other unvisited vertices can reduce $d[y]$. ## 3.5 Proof of Negative-Weight Failure Consider a graph with vertices $\{A, B, C\}$ and edges: $(A, B)$ with weight $1$, $(B, C)$ with weight $-3$, $(A, C)$ with weight $0$. - Dijkstra from $A$: visits $B$ with $d[B]=1$, then updates $d[C]=\min(0, 1+(-3)) = -2$. But Dijkstra would have **already finalized** $d[C] = 0$ after visiting $A$ (if $A$–$C$ is processed first), missing the negative path through $B$. This proves Dijkstra's algorithm **fails for negative edge weights**, and the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** must be used instead. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Input Graph — Mermaid Visualization > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram represents the **worked example graph** from Section 3.1. ```mermaid graph LR node1((1)) ---|2| node2((2)) node1((1)) ---|4| node3((3)) node2((2)) ---|1| node3((3)) node2((2)) ---|7| node4((4)) node3((3)) ---|3| node4((4)) node3((3)) ---|6| node5((5)) node4((4)) ---|2| node5((5)) style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style node3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style node4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style node5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 ``` **Legend:** **Gold** = Source vertex, **Light Blue** = Visited early, **Light Green** = Visited mid-stage, **Tomato** = Visited last. ## 4.2 Shortest Path Tree — Mermaid Visualization > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagram below shows the **final shortest-path tree (SPT)** rooted at source vertex $1$, reconstructed using the predecessor array. ```mermaid graph TD src((Source 1
