d v = infinity for all v not equal to s] B --> C[Initialize Priority Queue Q with all vertices] C --> D{Q is empty?} D -- No --> E[Extract vertex u with minimum d u from Q] E --> F[Mark u as visited: add to S] F --> G[For each unvisited neighbor v of u] G --> H{Is d u + w u v less than d v?} H -- Yes --> I[Update d v = d u + w u v
Set predecessor v = u] H -- No --> G I --> G G --> J{All neighbors processed?} J -- Yes --> D D -- Yes --> K[OUTPUT: Final distances d v and predecessors] K --> L[END] style A fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC style L fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC style E fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style H fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` ## 4.4 Iteration Snapshot Diagram > [!NOTE] > Visual representation of how the algorithm "expands" the visited set at each step. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ITER1["Iteration 1: Visit 1"] ITER1_NODES((1)) end subgraph ITER2["Iteration 2: Visit 2"] ITER2_NODES((1)) --- ITER2_NODES2((2)) end subgraph ITER3["Iteration 3: Visit 3"] ITER3_NODES((1)) --- ITER3_NODES2((2)) --- ITER3_NODES3((3)) end subgraph ITER4["Iteration 4: Visit 4"] ITER4_NODES((1)) --- ITER4_NODES2((2)) --- ITER4_NODES3((3)) --- ITER4_NODES4((4)) end subgraph ITER5["Iteration 5: Visit 5"] ITER5_NODES((1)) --- ITER5_NODES2((2)) --- ITER5_NODES3((3)) --- ITER5_NODES4((4)) --- ITER5_NODES5((5)) end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: State Dijkstra's algorithm. Under what condition does it fail to give the correct shortest path?** **Model Answer:** Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy single-source shortest path algorithm** that computes the minimum distance from a given source vertex $s$ to all other vertices in a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with **non-negative edge weights**. The algorithm maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose final shortest distances are known. Initially $S = \emptyset$ and all distances are $\infty$ except $d[s] = 0$. At each step, the vertex $u \notin S$ with the minimum $d[u]$ is added to $S$, and all edges from $u$ to unvisited neighbors are relaxed using the condition: $$ \text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \text{ then } d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v) $$ **Failing Condition:** Dijkstra's algorithm **fails when the graph contains negative edge weights**, i.e., when $w(u, v) < 0$ for some edge. The greedy property no longer holds because a path through an unvisited vertex could later become shorter after a negative-weight edge is traversed. In such cases, the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** must be used. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for stating the algorithm, 1 mark for the relaxation step, 1 mark for the failing condition with example. --- ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the concept of "edge relaxation" in Dijkstra's algorithm with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** **Edge Relaxation** is the process of attempting to improve the tentative shortest distance to a vertex $v$ by routing through an intermediate vertex $u$. Formally, for an edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$, the relaxation operation is: $$ \text{RELAX}(u, v): \quad \text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \text{ then } d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u, v) \text{ and } \pi[v] \leftarrow u $$ **Example:** Consider an edge from vertex $A$ to vertex $B$ with weight $w(A, B) = 5$. If $d[A] = 3$ and $d[B] = 10$, then the current path $A \to B$ has total weight $3 + 5 = 8$, which is less than $d[B] = 10$. After relaxation, $d[B] = 8$ and $\pi[B] = A$. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the inequality, 1 mark for the numerical example. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Module 3 Internal Choice Convention:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 3 long-answer questions (14 marks) carry an **internal choice** — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Both questions carry sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### 📘 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **Q: (a)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest path from vertex **$A$** to all other vertices in the following graph. Show the iteration table and final predecessor tree. **(7 marks)** **Edges and Weights:** - $A \to B: 4$, $A \to C: 2$ - $B \to C: 1$, $B \to D: 5$ - $C \to D: 8$, $C \to E: 10$ - $D \to E: 2$ #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): **Step 1: Initialize** - $d[A] = 0$, $d[B] = d[C] = d[D] = d[E] = \infty$, $S = \emptyset$ **Step 2: Iteration Table** | Iter | Visited $u$ | $d[A]$ | $d[B]$ | $d[C]$ | $d[D]$ | $d[E]$ | Update Notes | |:----:|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-------------| | Init | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | — | | 1 | **A** | 0 | **4** | **2** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\pi[B]=A$, $\pi[C]=A$ | | 2 | **C** | 0 | **3** | 2 | **10** | **12** | $\pi[B]=C$ (since $2+1<4$), $\pi[D]=C$, $\pi[E]=C$ | | 3 | **B** | 0 | 3 | 2 | **8** | 12 | $\pi[D]=B$ (since $3+5<10$) | | 4 | **D** | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | **10** | $\pi[E]=D$ (since $8+2<12$) | | 5 | **E** | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 10 | Final | **Final Shortest Distances:** $$ \delta(A, B) = 3, \quad \delta(A, C) = 2, \quad \delta(A, D) = 8, \quad \delta(A, E) = 10 $$ **Shortest Paths:** - $A \to B$: $A \to C \to B$ (weight $2 + 1 = 3$) - $A \to C$: $A \to C$ (weight $2$) - $A \to D$: $A \to C \to B \to D$ (weight $2 + 1 + 5 = 8$) - $A \to E$: $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ (weight $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$) --- **(b)** Explain the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm using (i) adjacency matrix and (ii) binary heap. Justify which is preferred for sparse graphs. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): **Time Complexity Analysis:** **(i) Using Adjacency Matrix (Naïve Implementation):** - The adjacency matrix requires $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ space and finding the minimum unvisited vertex requires scanning all $\vert V \vert$ vertices → $O(\vert V \vert)$ per extraction. - Total extractions: $\vert V \vert$, so vertex selection costs $O(\vert V \vert^2)$. - For each extracted vertex, all $\vert V \vert$ edges are scanned → $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ for relaxation. - **Total Time Complexity: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$** **(ii) Using Binary Min-Heap (Priority Queue):** - Building the heap: $O(\vert V \vert)$ - Extract-Min operation: $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ per vertex → total $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ for all extractions. - For each edge $(u, v)$, at most one Decrease-Key operation: $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ per edge → total $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$. - **Total Time Complexity: $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$** **Comparison Table:** | Implementation | Time Complexity | Best Suited For | |----------------|-----------------|------------------| | Adjacency Matrix | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | **Dense** graphs ($\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2$) | | Binary Heap | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | **Sparse** graphs ($\vert E \vert \ll \vert V \vert^2$) | **Justification for Sparse Graphs:** For sparse graphs, $\vert E \vert \approx O(\vert V \vert)$, so binary heap gives $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$, which is asymptotically much better than $O(\vert V \vert^2)$. Hence, **binary heap is preferred** for sparse graphs. --- ### 📗 Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` (Alternative Choice) **Q: (a)** Consider the directed graph with 6 vertices $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and the following edges: $A \to B$ (3), $A \to C$ (5), $B \to C$ (1), $B \to D$ (7), $C \to D$ (2), $C \to E$ (4), $D \to E$ (1), $D \to F$ (5), $E \to F$ (2). Apply Dijkstra's algorithm from source $A$ and determine the shortest path from $A$ to $F$. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): **Iteration Table:** | Iter | Visited $u$ | $d[A]$ | $d[B]$ | $d[C]$ | $d[D]$ | $d[E]$ | $d[F]$ | Update Notes | |:----:|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-------------| | Init | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | — | | 1 | **A** | 0 | **3** | **5** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\pi[B]=A$, $\pi[C]=A$ | | 2 | **B** | 0 | 3 | **4** | **10** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\pi[C]=B$ ($3+1<5$), $\pi[D]=B$ | | 3 | **C** | 0 | 3 | 4 | **6** | **8** | $\infty$ | $\pi[D]=C$ ($4+2<10$), $\pi[E]=C$ | | 4 | **D** | 0 | 3 | 4 | 6 | **7** | **11** | $\pi[E]=D$ ($6+1<8$), $\pi[F]=D$ | | 5 | **E** | 0 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 7 | **9** | $\pi[F]=E$ ($7+2<11$) | | 6 | **F** | 0 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 9 | Final | **Final Shortest Distances from $A$:** $$ \delta(A, B) = 3, \quad \delta(A, C) = 4, \quad \delta(A, D) = 6, \quad \delta(A, E) = 7, \quad \delta(A, F) = 9 $$ **Shortest Path from $A$ to $F$:** $A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F$ with total weight $3 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 9$. --- **(b)** "Dijkstra's algorithm cannot handle negative edge weights." Justify this statement with a counterexample. Compare Dijkstra's algorithm with the Bellman-Ford algorithm in terms of applicability and complexity. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): **Counterexample Demonstrating Failure:** Consider the graph: $A \to B$ (weight 1), $B \to C$ (weight $-3$), $A \to C$ (weight 2). - Dijkstra initializes: $d[A]=0, d[B]=\infty, d[C]=\infty$ - Step 1: Visit $A$, relax $A \to C$: $d[C] = 2$, $\pi[C] = A$ - Step 2: Visit $C$ (since $d[C] = 2 < d[B] = 1$... wait, $d[B] = 1$ is smaller, so visit $B$ first) - Step 2 (corrected): Visit $B$ with $d[B] = 1$, relax $B \to C$: $d[C] = \min(2, 1 + (-3)) = -2$. Update $d[C] = -2$. In this case, Dijkstra **does** find the correct answer if $B$ is processed before $C$. But consider a different graph: $A \to B$ (weight 1), $B \to C$ (weight $-2$), $A \to C$ (weight 3). - Visit $A$, relax $A \to B$: $d[B] = 1$, $\pi[B] = A$; relax $A \to C$: $d[C] = 3$, $\pi[C] = A$. - Visit $B$ (min = 1), relax $B \to C$: $d[C] = \min(3, 1 + (-2)) = -1$. **Here Dijkstra works.** The **classical counterexample** where Dijkstra fails: $A \to B$ (weight 2), $B \to C$ (weight $-4$), $A \to C$ (weight 1). - Visit $A$, relax edges: $d[B] = 2$, $d[C] = 1$. - Visit $C$ (min unvisited = 1), **mark $C$ as finalized** with $d[C] = 1$. (Here $\delta(A, C) = 1$ is incorrect; the true shortest path is $A \to B \to C = 2 + (-4) = -2$.) This proves that **once Dijkstra finalizes a vertex, it never reconsiders it**, which fails with negative weights. **Comparison Table — Dijkstra vs. Bellman-Ford:** | Parameter | Dijkstra's Algorithm | Bellman-Ford Algorithm | |-----------|----------------------|------------------------| | **Edge Weights** | Non-negative only | Can handle negative weights | | **Negative Cycles** | Not detected | Detected | | **Approach** | Greedy (vertex-by-vertex) | Dynamic Programming (edge-by-edge relaxation) | | **Time Complexity** | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with heap | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | | **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | | **Use Case** | GPS, network routing (OSPF) | Currency arbitrage, financial models | | **Faster?** | Yes (faster) | Slower but more general | **Justification Summary:** Dijkstra's algorithm exploits the **greedy choice property** which fundamentally relies on the **non-negativity** of edge weights. When negative weights exist, the triangle inequality is violated for unvisited vertices, and the algorithm can prematurely finalize a vertex with a sub-optimal distance. The Bellman-Ford algorithm avoids this by performing $\vert V \vert - 1$ rounds of edge relaxation, ensuring all possible paths are considered. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** > > 1. **Mark Loss Cause #1 — Wrong Source Vertex:** Students often mistakenly start the algorithm from a different vertex than specified. Always **highlight the source vertex in your first iteration row**. > > 2. **Mark Loss Cause #2 — Forgetting to Update Predecessors:** The predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$ is essential for path reconstruction. Failing to update $\pi[v]$ when relaxing edges costs **at least 1–2 marks**. > > 3. **Mark Loss Cause #3 — Not Finalizing Visited Set:** Each vertex should be **explicitly marked as visited** (added to $S$) once extracted. Skipping this visualization is a common error. > > 4. **Mark Loss Cause #4 — Confusing "Tentative" with "Final" Distances:** Use $\infty$ for uninitialized values and **bold the value being finalized** in each iteration row. > > 5. **Mark Loss Cause #5 — Time Complexity Misquotation:** Writing $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ without the $\vert V \vert$ term for vertex extraction is incomplete. Always include the full expression. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - ✅ **Dijkstra's algorithm** solves the **single-source shortest path problem** on graphs with **non-negative edge weights**. - ✅ It is a **greedy algorithm** — once a vertex is finalized, its distance is never updated. - ✅ The **core operation** is *edge relaxation*: `if d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) then d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v)`. - ✅ Initial distances: $d[s] = 0$ and $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. ### 🧠 Algorithm Steps (Memorize) - ✅ **Step 1:** Initialize distances and visited set. - ✅ **Step 2:** Pick the unvisited vertex with minimum $d[\cdot]$. - ✅ **Step 3:** Mark it visited and relax all outgoing edges to unvisited neighbors. - ✅ **Step 4:** Repeat until all vertices are visited (or priority queue is empty). - ✅ **Step 5:** Reconstruct shortest paths using the predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$. ### ⏱️ Time Complexities (Three Versions) - ✅ **Adjacency Matrix / Linear Search:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ - ✅ **Binary Min-Heap:** $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ - ✅ **Fibonacci Heap (Theoretical Optimum):** $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ ### ⚠️ Key Restrictions - ❌ **Cannot handle negative edge weights** — use **Bellman-Ford** instead. - ❌ **Cannot detect negative cycles** — use **Bellman-Ford** for this purpose. - ✅ Works on both **directed and undirected** graphs (with non-negative weights). - ✅ Produces a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the source. ### 📐 Important Properties - ✅ **Triangle Inequality:** $\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ for all $u, v$. - ✅ **Upper-Bound Property:** $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ is an invariant. - ✅ **Convergence Property:** When $u$ is extracted, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. - ✅ **Path Reconstruction:** Use $\pi[v]$ recursively: $\text{print-path}(s, v) = \text{print-path}(s, \pi[v]) \to v$. ### 🎯 Exam-Specific Tips - 📝 Always draw the **iteration table** with explicit visited set, distances, and predecessor updates. - 📝 **Bold** the newly finalized value in each row for clarity. - 📝 State the **time complexity** and **restrictions** explicitly in long answers. - 📝 When asked to find a **path to a specific vertex**, the algorithm can **terminate early** once that vertex is extracted. - 📝 Practice drawing the **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** alongside the iteration table. ### 🔗 Real-World Connections (For "Application-Type" Questions) - 🌐 **OSPF Protocol** in computer networks uses Dijkstra's algorithm. - 🚗 **Google Maps** uses a variant of Dijkstra (or A*) for route planning. - 💰 **Foreign Exchange (Forex) Arbitrage** detection uses Bellman-Ford (negative cycles = arbitrage opportunity). - 🎮 **Video Game AI** uses A* (Dijkstra + heuristic) for NPC pathfinding.

topics.title: Floyd-Warshall Shortest Path Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floyd-Warshall Shortest Path Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Floyd-Warshall Algorithm** is an all-pairs shortest path algorithm used in weighted graphs. It computes the shortest path distances between **every pair of vertices** in a single execution. It is a classic application of **Dynamic Programming (DP)** where the shortest path between any two vertices $(i, j)$ is constructed by progressively considering each intermediate vertex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEVT523 – Module 3):**
> The Floyd-Warshall algorithm finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted directed graph. It works for graphs with positive or negative edge weights, but no negative-weight cycles. The time complexity is $O(V^3)$ and the space complexity is $O(V^2)$.

Mathematically, the algorithm maintains a sequence of distance matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, \ldots, D^{(n)}$, where each $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ represents the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using **only vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$** as intermediate vertices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Travel Network Optimizer"**
>
> Imagine a flight booking website that must compute the **cheapest ticket between every pair of cities** in a country. Direct flights are known, but two-stop or three-stop connections might be cheaper. The Floyd-Warshall algorithm works exactly like a systematic search:
> 1. **Stage 1:** Allow 0 intermediate stops (use only direct edges).
> 2. **Stage 2:** Now also allow stops via City #1.
> 3. **Stage 3:** Allow stops via Cities #1 and #2.
> 4. ... and so on, until every city is allowed as a potential waypoint.
>
> At each stage, the algorithm checks: *"Is it cheaper to fly $i \rightarrow j$ directly, or via an already-permitted city $k$?"* — if via $k$ is cheaper, update the price. After $n$ stages, every shortest route is known.

The two key mathematical components are:
- $D[i][j]$ → the **shortest distance** from $i$ to $j$ (cost matrix).
- $P[i][j]$ → the **predecessor matrix** to reconstruct the actual path (route matrix).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "All-Pairs" and not "Single-Source"?**
> Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from **one source** to all others. Bellman-Ford does the same. Floyd-Warshall, in contrast, solves the **all-pairs** problem in one shot. It is the natural choice for dense graphs where you need every pair of distances, e.g., in **network routing tables** (used in OSPF protocol on the internet) and **graph density analysis**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Time Complexity:** $O(V^3)$ — three nested loops over vertices.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(V^2)$ — for the distance matrix.
- **Operative Constant:** No arbitrary constants; the algorithm is purely combinatorial.
- **Negative Cycle Detection:** If $D[i][i] < 0$ for any $i$ after the algorithm terminates, a **negative cycle** exists in the graph.
- **Applicable Graph Types:** Directed, undirected (treating each undirected edge as two directed edges), weighted (positive or negative, but no negative cycles).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Recurrence Relation (The Heart of the Algorithm)

The algorithm is built upon a single elegant recurrence:

$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \left( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right)$$

### 2.1.1 Interpretation of the Recurrence

- **First term $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$:** The shortest known path from $i$ to $j$ using only vertices $\{1, 2, \ldots, k-1\}$ as intermediates.
- **Second term $D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$:** A potentially shorter path that **passes through vertex $k$** as an intermediate stop.
- **The $\min$ operator:** Picks whichever of the two is shorter.

### 2.1.2 Why This Works (The DP Insight)

- **Base case ($k=0$):** $D^{(0)}[i][j]$ is the weight of the direct edge $(i, j)$ if it exists, or $\infty$ if not. The diagonal $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$.
- **Inductive step:** Suppose we already know all shortest paths using intermediates from $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$. Now we introduce vertex $k$. Any shortest path from $i$ to $j$ that uses vertex $k$ must pass through $k$ exactly once on the "interior" of the path (i.e., the subpath $i \rightarrow k$ and $k \rightarrow j$ uses only $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$).
- **Termination:** After $k = n$ iterations, $D^{(n)}[i][j]$ is the absolute shortest path from $i$ to $j$.

## 2.2 Algorithm Steps (Logic Flow)

1. **Initialize** the distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ of size $V \times V$:
   - $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j)$ if edge $(i, j)$ exists.
   - $D^{(0)}[i][j] = \infty$ if no edge exists.
   - $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ for all $i$.
2. **Optional:** Initialize the predecessor matrix $P[i][j] = i$ or $P[i][j] = j$ (varies by implementation).
3. **Outer Loop** (k = 1 to n): For each vertex $k$ as a potential intermediate.
4. **Middle Loop** (i = 1 to n): For each source vertex $i$.
5. **Inner Loop** (j = 1 to n): For each destination vertex $j$.
6. **Update:** If $D[i][k] + D[k][j] < D[i][j]$, then $D[i][j] = D[i][k] + D[k][j]$, and update predecessor: $P[i][j] = P[k][j]$.
7. **Check for Negative Cycles:** After all updates, if $D[i][i] < 0$ for any $i$, a negative-weight cycle exists.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula & Notation Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|
| $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j)$ | Initial weight of edge $(i, j)$ | $D^{(0)}[i][j] = \infty$ if no edge |
| $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ | Distance from a vertex to itself | Always 0 |
| $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ | Core DP recurrence | $k$ is the new intermediate vertex |
| Time Complexity $= O(V^3)$ | Three nested loops | Independent of number of edges $E$ |
| Space Complexity $= O(V^2)$ | Distance + Predecessor matrices | Optimal for dense graphs |
| $D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0$ | Detects negative-weight cycle | If true, no shortest path exists |
| $P[i][j] = k$ | Predecessor of $j$ on shortest path from $i$ | Used for path reconstruction |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** Always use $\infty$ (or a very large number like 9999) for non-existent edges in the initialization step. KTU exam answers without the initialization matrix $D^{(0)}$ explicitly shown usually **lose 2 marks**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Internet Routing (OSPF Protocol):** Routers precompute the shortest path to every other router in their network.
- **Robotics & Path Planning:** Multi-agent navigation where each robot needs distances to all other robots.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Finding optimal multi-hop delivery routes in logistics networks.
- **Social Network Analysis:** Computing the "closeness" of every user to every other user (a measure of centrality).
- **VLSI Design:** Computing wire-lengths between all pairs of components for optimal layout.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a directed weighted graph with **$V = 4$** vertices: $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

The directed edge weights are:

| From $\downarrow$ / To $\rightarrow$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 3 | 10 | $\infty$ |
| **2** | $\infty$ | 0 | $\infty$ | 2 |
| **3** | $\infty$ | 1 | 0 | $\infty$ |
| **4** | 4 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 0 |

> **Reading the table:** Row 1 says — $1 \rightarrow 1$ costs 0, $1 \rightarrow 2$ costs 3, $1 \rightarrow 3$ costs 10, $1 \rightarrow 4$ has no direct edge (so $\infty$).

### 3.1.2 Step 0 — Initialize $D^{(0)}$

This is just the adjacency matrix shown above, with the diagonal set to 0 and missing edges to $\infty$.

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 10 & \infty \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 1 & 0 & \infty \\
4 & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 1 — Introduce Vertex 1 as Intermediate ($k = 1$)

For every pair $(i, j)$, check: Is $D[i][1] + D[1][j] < D[i][j]$?

- $D[2][1] = \infty$, so $D[2][1] + D[1][j] = \infty$. No updates via vertex 1 from row 2.
- $D[3][1] = \infty$, so no updates via vertex 1 from row 3.
- $D[4][1] = 4$. So $D[4][1] + D[1][j]$ means $4 + D[1][j]$:
  - $D[4][2] = \min(\infty, 4 + 3) = 7$
  - $D[4][3] = \min(\infty, 4 + 10) = 14$
  - $D[4][4] = \min(0, 4 + \infty) = 0$ (unchanged)

All other entries are already smaller than going via vertex 1.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 10 & \infty \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 1 & 0 & \infty \\
4 & 7 & 14 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 2 — Introduce Vertex 2 as Intermediate ($k = 2$)

Now check for all $(i, j)$: Is $D[i][2] + D[2][j] < D[i][j]$?

- From row 1: $D[1][2] = 3$.
  - $D[1][4] = \min(\infty, 3 + 2) = 5$ ✓
  - $D[1][3] = \min(10, 3 + \infty) = 10$ (unchanged)
- From row 3: $D[3][2] = 1$.
  - $D[3][4] = \min(\infty, 1 + 2) = 3$ ✓
- From row 4: $D[4][2] = 7$.
  - $D[4][4] = \min(0, 7 + 2) = 0$ (unchanged)
  - $D[4][3] = \min(14, 7 + \infty) = 14$ (unchanged)

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 10 & 5 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 1 & 0 & 3 \\
4 & 7 & 14 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 3 — Introduce Vertex 3 as Intermediate ($k = 3$)

Check: Is $D[i][3] + D[3][j] < D[i][j]$?

- From row 1: $D[1][3] = 10$.
  - $D[1][4] = \min(5, 10 + 3) = 5$ (unchanged, $5 < 13$)
  - $D[1][2] = \min(3, 10 + 1) = 3$ (unchanged, $3 < 11$)
- From row 2: $D[2][3] = \infty$. No updates.
- From row 4: $D[4][3] = 14$.
  - $D[4][2] = \min(7, 14 + 1) = 7$ (unchanged, $7 < 15$)
  - $D[4][4] = \min(0, 14 + 3) = 0$ (unchanged)

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 10 & 5 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 1 & 0 & 3 \\
4 & 7 & 14 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

No change — vertex 3 didn't help any path here.

### 3.1.6 Step 4 — Introduce Vertex 4 as Intermediate ($k = 4$)

Check: Is $D[i][4] + D[4][j] < D[i][j]$?

- From row 1: $D[1][4] = 5$.
  - $D[1][2] = \min(3, 5 + 7) = 3$ (unchanged, $3 < 12$)
  - $D[1][3] = \min(10, 5 + 14) = 10$ (unchanged, $10 < 19$)
- From row 2: $D[2][4] = 2$.
  - $D[2][1] = \min(\infty, 2 + 4) = 6$ ✓ **Major update!**
  - $D[2][3] = \min(\infty, 2 + 14) = 16$ ✓
  - $D[2][2] = \min(0, 2 + 7) = 0$ (unchanged)
- From row 3: $D[3][4] = 3$.
  - $D[3][1] = \min(\infty, 3 + 4) = 7$ ✓
  - $D[3][3] = \min(0, 3 + 14) = 0$ (unchanged)

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 10 & 5 \\
6 & 0 & 16 & 2 \\
7 & 1 & 0 & 3 \\
4 & 7 & 14 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.7 Final Result

The final distance matrix $D^{(4)}$ gives the shortest path between every pair of vertices. For example:
- Shortest path $1 \rightarrow 4$ has cost **5** (route: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4$, costing $3 + 2 = 5$).
- Shortest path $2 \rightarrow 1$ has cost **6** (route: $2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1$, costing $2 + 4 = 6$).
- Shortest path $3 \rightarrow 1$ has cost **7** (route: $3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1$, costing $3 + 4 = 7$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Cycle Check:** All diagonal entries $D^{(4)}[i][i] = 0$. No negative-weight cycle exists, so the algorithm is valid.

## 3.2 Algebraic Justification of the Recurrence

The mathematical correctness of the recurrence

$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \left( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right)$$

can be proven by induction on $k$:

\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Base case } (k=0): \\
&D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j) \quad \text{if edge exists, else } \infty, \quad D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0 \\
&\text{Trivially correct: shortest path using zero intermediates is the direct edge.} \\
& \\
&\textbf{Inductive hypothesis:} \\
&D^{(k-1)}[i][j] \text{ is the shortest path from } i \text{ to } j \text{ using intermediates } \subseteq \{1, \ldots, k-1\}. \\
& \\
&\textbf{Inductive step:} \text{Any path from } i \text{ to } j \text{ using intermediates } \subseteq \{1, \ldots, k\} \text{ either:} \\
&\quad \textbf{Case A:} \text{Does not use } k \text{ as intermediate.} \Rightarrow \text{Cost} = D^{(k-1)}[i][j]. \\
&\quad \textbf{Case B:} \text{Uses } k \text{ as intermediate.} \Rightarrow \text{Path splits as } i \rightsquigarrow k \rightsquigarrow j, \\
&\quad \text{with cost } D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]. \\
&\text{Taking the minimum covers both cases, so } D^{(k)}[i][j] \text{ is optimal.}
\end{aligned}

## 3.3 Comparison Table with Other Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Time Complexity | Negative Weights | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dijkstra** | Single-Source | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | ❌ No | Non-negative weights, one source |
| **Bellman-Ford** | Single-Source | $O(V \cdot E)$ | ✅ Yes | One source, negative weights allowed |
| **Floyd-Warshall** | All-Pairs | $O(V^3)$ | ✅ Yes (no neg. cycle) | All pairs needed; dense graphs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart of the Floyd-Warshall Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> initB[Initialize Distance Matrix D with edge weights]
    initB --> initC[Set D i i = 0 and D i j = infinity for missing edges]
    initC --> initD[Optional: Initialize Predecessor Matrix P]
    initD --> loopK1[For k = 1 to n vertices]
    loopK1 --> loopI1[For i = 1 to n vertices]
    loopI1 --> loopJ1[For j = 1 to n vertices]
    loopJ1 --> checkCond{D i k plus D k j less than D i j}
    checkCond -- Yes --> updateOp[Update D i j = D i k + D k j and set P i j = P k j]
    checkCond -- No --> keepOld[Keep current D i j unchanged]
    updateOp --> nextJ[Next j]
    keepOld --> nextJ
    nextJ --> loopJ1
    loopJ1 --> nextI[Next i]
    nextI --> loopI1
    loopI1 --> nextK[Next k]
    nextK --> loopK1
    loopK1 --> negCycleCheck{Any D i i less than 0}
    negCycleCheck -- Yes --> negCycleFound[Negative cycle detected. No valid shortest paths]
    negCycleCheck -- No --> finalD[Final D is the all pairs shortest distance matrix]
    negCycleFound --> stopZ([Stop])
    finalD --> reconstruct[Use P to reconstruct any shortest path]
    reconstruct --> stopZ
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        G[Weighted Directed Graph G = V E]
    end
    subgraph INIT["Initialization Block"]
        D0["Build D to the power of 0 from adjacency matrix"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Dynamic Programming Core"]
        K1["Iteration k = 1: permit vertex 1 as intermediate"]
        K2["Iteration k = 2: permit vertex 2 as intermediate"]
        K3["..."]
        KN["Iteration k = n: permit vertex n as intermediate"]
    end
    subgraph VERIFY["Verification Block"]
        CHECK["Check diagonal of D to the power of n for negative values"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        DFIN["Final All Pairs Shortest Distance Matrix"]
        PREC["Predecessor Matrix for path reconstruction"]
    end
    G --> D0
    D0 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> KN
    KN --> CHECK
    CHECK --> DFIN
    CHECK --> PREC
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Diagram of One Cell Update

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Current D i j value] --> decision{Is i equal to k or j equal to k}
    decision -- Yes --> node2[No update required since k cannot be used as own intermediate]
    decision -- No --> calc[Compute candidate cost equals D i k plus D k j]
    calc --> compare{Candidate less than current D i j}
    compare -- Yes --> node3[Update D i j to candidate cost and set P i j equals P k j]
    compare -- No --> node4[Retain current D i j]
    node2 --> node5[Move to next j]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the recurrence relation used in the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. What is its time complexity?**

**Model Answer:**

The Floyd-Warshall algorithm uses the following dynamic programming recurrence:

$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \left( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right)$$

where $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ denotes the shortest path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only vertices $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ as intermediate vertices. The time complexity is $O(V^3)$ and space complexity is $O(V^2)$.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: How does the Floyd-Warshall algorithm detect the presence of a negative-weight cycle in a graph?**

**Model Answer:**

After the Floyd-Warshall algorithm completes all $n$ iterations, the final distance matrix $D^{(n)}$ is examined. If for any vertex $i$, the diagonal entry $D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0$, then a negative-weight cycle exists in the graph (because a non-zero path of negative total cost has been found from $i$ back to $i$, which is only possible if a negative cycle was traversed). In such a case, the shortest path problem has no well-defined solution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with sub-parts of 7 marks each, and students must answer **either Option A or Option B** (Internal Choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Apply the Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find the all-pairs shortest paths for the following directed weighted graph with 4 vertices.**

The adjacency (weight) matrix is:

| | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 5 | $\infty$ | 6 |
| **2** | $\infty$ | 0 | 3 | $\infty$ |
| **3** | 2 | $\infty$ | 0 | 4 |
| **4** | $\infty$ | 7 | $\infty$ | 0 |

#### **Part (a) (7 Marks):** Show the initial matrix $D^{(0)}$ and the matrix after introducing vertex 1 as an intermediate (i.e., compute $D^{(1)}$).

**Solution:**

**Initial Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & \infty & 6 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
2 & \infty & 0 & 4 \\
\infty & 7 & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Computing $D^{(1)}$ (k = 1):** We check for every pair $(i, j)$ whether $D[i][1] + D[1][j] < D[i][j]$.

- $D[2][1] = \infty$ → no updates originating from row 2 via vertex 1.
- $D[3][1] = 2$ → check: $D[3][2] = \min(\infty, 2 + 5) = 7$ ✓, $D[3][4] = \min(4, 2 + 6) = 4$ (unchanged, $4 < 8$).
- $D[4][1] = \infty$ → no updates from row 4.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & \infty & 6 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
2 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\
\infty & 7 & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### **Part (b) (7 Marks):** Compute $D^{(2)}$, $D^{(3)}$, and $D^{(4)}$ and present the final all-pairs shortest path matrix.

**Solution:**

**$D^{(2)}$ (k = 2):** Check $D[i][2] + D[2][j] < D[i][j]$ for all $(i, j)$.

- Row 1 ($D[1][2]=5$): $D[1][3] = \min(\infty, 5 + 3) = 8$ ✓, $D[1][4] = \min(6, 5 + \infty) = 6$ (unchanged).
- Row 3 ($D[3][2]=7$): $D[3][3] = \min(0, 7 + 3) = 0$ (unchanged), $D[3][4] = \min(4, 7 + \infty) = 4$ (unchanged).
- Row 4 ($D[4][2]=7$): $D[4][3] = \min(\infty, 7 + 3) = 10$ ✓.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 6 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
2 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\
\infty & 7 & 10 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(3)}$ (k = 3):** Check $D[i][3] + D[3][j] < D[i][j]$ for all $(i, j)$.

- Row 1 ($D[1][3]=8$): $D[1][2] = \min(5, 8 + 7) = 5$ (unchanged), $D[1][4] = \min(6, 8 + 4) = 6$ (unchanged, $6 < 12$).
- Row 2 ($D[2][3]=3$): $D[2][1] = \min(\infty, 3 + 2) = 5$ ✓, $D[2][4] = \min(\infty, 3 + 4) = 7$ ✓.
- Row 4 ($D[4][3]=10$): $D[4][1] = \min(\infty, 10 + 2) = 12$ ✓, $D[4][4] = \min(0, 10 + 4) = 0$ (unchanged).

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 3 & 7 \\
2 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\
12 & 7 & 10 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(4)}$ (k = 4):** Check $D[i][4] + D[4][j] < D[i][j]$ for all $(i, j)$.

- Row 1 ($D[1][4]=6$): $D[1][2] = \min(5, 6 + 7) = 5$ (unchanged), $D[1][3] = \min(8, 6 + 10) = 8$ (unchanged, $8 < 16$).
- Row 2 ($D[2][4]=7$): $D[2][1] = \min(5, 7 + 12) = 5$ (unchanged, $5 < 19$), $D[2][2] = \min(0, 7 + 7) = 0$ (unchanged), $D[2][3] = \min(3, 7 + 10) = 3$ (unchanged, $3 < 17$).
- Row 3 ($D[3][4]=4$): $D[3][1] = \min(2, 4 + 12) = 2$ (unchanged, $2 < 16$), $D[3][2] = \min(7, 4 + 7) = 7$ (unchanged, $7 < 11$).

**Final Matrix:**

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 3 & 7 \\
2 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\
12 & 7 & 10 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**All diagonal entries are zero, so no negative cycle exists.** The shortest paths are:
- $1 \rightarrow 4$: cost **6** (direct).
- $2 \rightarrow 1$: cost **5** (route: $2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$, costing $3 + 2$).
- $4 \rightarrow 1$: cost **12** (route: $4 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$, costing $7 + 3 + 2$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Find the all-pairs shortest paths using the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for the graph with the following adjacency matrix.**

| | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 4 | 11 |
| **2** | 6 | 0 | 2 |
| **3** | 3 | $\infty$ | 0 |

#### **Part (a) (7 Marks):** Compute $D^{(0)}$ and $D^{(1)}$ and explain the steps.

**Solution:**

**Initial Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 11 \\
6 & 0 & 2 \\
3 & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Computing $D^{(1)}$ (k = 1):** Check $D[i][1] + D[1][j] < D[i][j]$.

- Row 2 ($D[2][1]=6$): $D[2][2] = \min(0, 6 + 4) = 0$ (unchanged), $D[2][3] = \min(2, 6 + 11) = 2$ (unchanged, $2 < 17$).
- Row 3 ($D[3][1]=3$): $D[3][2] = \min(\infty, 3 + 4) = 7$ ✓.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 11 \\
6 & 0 & 2 \\
3 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### **Part (b) (7 Marks):** Compute $D^{(2)}$ and $D^{(3)}$ and present the final matrix with all shortest paths.

**Solution:**

**$D^{(2)}$ (k = 2):** Check $D[i][2] + D[2][j] < D[i][j]$.

- Row 1 ($D[1][2]=4$): $D[1][3] = \min(11, 4 + 2) = 6$ ✓.
- Row 3 ($D[3][2]=7$): $D[3][3] = \min(0, 7 + 2) = 0$ (unchanged), $D[3][1] = \min(3, 7 + 6) = 3$ (unchanged, $3 < 13$).

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 6 \\
6 & 0 & 2 \\
3 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(3)}$ (k = 3):** Check $D[i][3] + D[3][j] < D[i][j]$.

- Row 1 ($D[1][3]=6$): $D[1][1] = \min(0, 6 + 3) = 0$ (unchanged), $D[1][2] = \min(4, 6 + 7) = 4$ (unchanged, $4 < 13$).
- Row 2 ($D[2][3]=2$): $D[2][1] = \min(6, 2 + 3) = 5$ ✓, $D[2][2] = \min(0, 2 + 7) = 0$ (unchanged).

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 2 \\
3 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final All-Pairs Shortest Path Matrix:**

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 2 \\
3 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**All diagonal entries equal 0 → no negative cycles.** The shortest paths are:
- $1 \rightarrow 3$: cost **6** (via $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$, costing $4 + 2$).
- $2 \rightarrow 1$: cost **5** (via $2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$, costing $2 + 3$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting to show $D^{(0)}$ explicitly** — Examiners expect the initialization step. Skipping it costs **1–2 marks** easily.
> 2. **Using $\infty$ incorrectly in the sum** — When computing $D[i][k] + D[k][j]$, if either is $\infty$, the sum is $\infty$. Many students incorrectly add the numbers. **Always treat $\infty$ as absorbing.**
> 3. **Order of loops confusion** — The OUTER loop must be $k$ (intermediate vertex), NOT $i$ or $j$. Misnaming the loop variable structure loses 2 marks in KTU exams.
> 4. **Forgetting the negative cycle check** — After the final matrix, the answer should explicitly state the check $D[i][i] < 0$.
> 5. **Not showing step-by-step matrices** — KTU 2024 Scheme requires showing every intermediate matrix $D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, \ldots, D^{(n)}$. Jumping directly to the final answer loses the bulk of the marks.
> 6. **Confusing Floyd-Warshall with Dijkstra** — Dijkstra is single-source, Floyd-Warshall is all-pairs. Mixing them up in definitions costs the first sub-part marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Floyd-Warshall Algorithm (Module 3)**

- **Type:** All-Pairs Shortest Path algorithm (Dynamic Programming based).
- **Recurrence:** $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(V^3)$.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(V^2)$ — uses a single distance matrix that is updated in-place.
- **Initialization:** $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$, $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j)$ if edge exists, else $\infty$.
- **Negative Weights:** Allowed ✅; **Negative Cycles:** Not allowed ❌ (detected via $D[i][i] < 0$).
- **Loop Order:** $k$ outermost (intermediate), then $i$ (source), then $j$ (destination).
- **Best for:** Dense graphs where you need shortest paths between all pairs.
- **Worst for:** Sparse single-source problems (Dijkstra is more efficient).
- **Predecessor Matrix:** Maintained alongside distance matrix for path reconstruction.
- **KTU 2024 Most Common Question:** Apply Floyd-Warshall to a 3-vertex or 4-vertex graph with sub-parts asking for $D^{(1)}$, $D^{(2)}$, ..., $D^{(n)}$ and the final all-pairs matrix.
- **Key Mnemonic:** "**I**ntermediate **k**" — think of $k$ as the *newly allowed layover city* at each stage.
- **Examiner's Favourite Trap:** Failing to show intermediate matrices; treating $\infty + x$ as a finite number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertex Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vertex Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — VERTEX CONNECTIVITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with $|V| \geq 2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertex Connectivity ($\kappa(G)$):**
> The **vertex connectivity** of a connected graph $G$, denoted by $\kappa(G)$ (kappa of $G$), is the **minimum number of vertices** whose removal (along with all incident edges) results in a graph that is either **disconnected** or reduced to a **trivial single-vertex graph $K_1$**.

Mathematically,

$$\kappa(G) = \min \bigl\{ \vert S \vert \;:\; S \subseteq V(G) \text{ and } G - S \text{ is disconnected or } K_1 \bigr\}$$

Such a minimum set $S$ is called a **vertex cut** of $G$.

### 1.2 Auxiliary Definitions (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Vertex Cut** | A set $S \subseteq V$ whose removal disconnects the graph |
| **Cut Vertex (Articulation Point)** | A vertex cut of size **1** |
| **Non-cut Vertex** | A vertex whose removal leaves the graph connected |
| **Separating Set** | A set whose removal disconnects two specific vertices $u, v$ |
| **Internally Vertex-Disjoint Paths** | Paths between $u, v$ that share no internal vertex |

### 1.3 Special Boundary Cases

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Values (KTU Board-Favorite):**
> - If $G$ is **disconnected** or $K_1$ (single vertex): $\kappa(G) = 0$
> - If $G = K_2$ (single edge): $\kappa(G) = 1$
> - If $G = K_n$ (complete graph, $n \geq 2$): $\kappa(K_n) = n - 1$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a graph as the **road map of a city**. Each vertex is a major town, and each edge is a highway. Now imagine an enemy wants to **cut off communication** between regions by destroying the fewest towns possible.

- The **vertex connectivity** $\kappa(G)$ is the **smallest number of towns** the enemy must destroy to split the city into disconnected parts.
- If removing **just one town** isolates a region, that town is called a **cut vertex (articulation point)**.
- A graph with $\kappa(G) = 2$ means the enemy needs to destroy **at least two towns** — the network is more robust.
- The **complete graph $K_n$** is the most robust: even after destroying $n-2$ towns, every remaining pair is still directly linked.

This robustness metric is exactly what engineers care about in **reliable network design, fault-tolerant systems, and parallel computing architectures**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of $\kappa(G)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These properties appear in nearly every KTU university exam. Memorize them thoroughly.

1. **Whitney's Inequality** (Most Important — Mandatory for KTU):
$$\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$
where $\lambda(G)$ = edge connectivity, $\delta(G)$ = minimum degree.

2. **Upper Bound:**
$$\kappa(G) \leq n - 1 \quad \text{for any graph with } n \geq 2$$

3. **Complete Graph Bound:**
$$\kappa(K_n) = n - 1$$

4. **Cycle Graph:**
$$\kappa(C_n) = 2 \quad \text{for } n \geq 3$$

5. **Path Graph:**
$$\kappa(P_n) = 1 \quad \text{for } n \geq 2$$

6. **Trees with $n \geq 2$:**
$$\kappa(T) = 1$$ (every tree with at least two vertices has a cut vertex).

7. **Bipartite/Complete Bipartite $K_{m,n}$:**
$$\kappa(K_{m,n}) = \min(m, n)$$

## 2.2 Menger's Theorem (Vertex Version) — Board Examiner Favorite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Menger's Theorem (Vertex Form):**
> Let $G$ be a connected graph and $u, v$ be two non-adjacent vertices. Then the **maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $u$–$v$ paths** equals the **minimum number of vertices** whose removal separates $u$ from $v$.

Equivalently, for any graph:
$$\kappa(G) = \min_{u, v \in V} \, \bigl( \text{max number of internally disjoint } u \text{-}v \text{ paths} \bigr)$$

## 2.3 Dirac-Type Sufficient Conditions

- If $\delta(G) \geq k$ and $|V| \geq k + 1$, then $\kappa(G) \geq k$ (for $k \leq 2$); for higher $k$, additional structural conditions apply.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunication Networks** | Minimum node failures to cripple a network |
| **VLSI Chip Design** | Fault tolerance of circuit interconnects |
| **Transportation Systems** | Critical hub identification |
| **Social Networks** | Influence/bridging community detection |
| **Distributed Computing** | Redundancy in cluster topologies |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠️ Markdown safeguard: All absolute values use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table syntax breakage.

| Graph Type | $\kappa(G)$ |
|---|---|
| $K_1$ (single vertex) | $0$ |
| Disconnected graph | $0$ |
| $K_n$ (complete, $n \geq 2$) | $n - 1$ |
| $K_{m,n}$ (complete bipartite) | $\min(m, n)$ |
| $C_n$ (cycle, $n \geq 3$) | $2$ |
| $P_n$ (path, $n \geq 2$) | $1$ |
| Tree (with $\geq 2$ vertices) | $1$ |
| Petersen graph | $3$ |
| $n$-cube $Q_n$ | $n$ |
| Wheel $W_n$ ($n \geq 4$) | $3$ |

**Whitney's Master Chain (always true):**

$$\boxed{\kappa(G) \;\leq\; \lambda(G) \;\leq\; \delta(G)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Finding $\kappa(G)$ of a Custom Graph

**Problem:** Compute the vertex connectivity of the graph $G$ shown below.

Vertices: $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
Edges: $E = \{12, 23, 34, 45, 15, 13\}$

### Step 1 — Compute the degree of each vertex

$$\deg(1) = 3, \quad \deg(2) = 2, \quad \deg(3) = 3, \quad \deg(4) = 2, \quad \deg(5) = 2$$

### Step 2 — Find the minimum degree

$$\delta(G) = \min(3, 2, 3, 2, 2) = 2$$

### Step 3 — Apply the upper bound from Whitney's inequality

$$\kappa(G) \leq \delta(G) = 2$$

### Step 4 — Test whether $\kappa(G) = 1$ by hunting for a cut vertex

Try removing vertex **3**:
After removing $3$, remaining edges are $\{12, 45, 15\}$. The graph splits into components $\{1, 2, 5\}$ and $\{4\}$. Hence vertex $3$ **is a cut vertex**.

### Step 5 — Conclude

Since a cut vertex exists, $\kappa(G) = 1$.

$$\boxed{\kappa(G) = 1}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — $\kappa(K_{m,n})$

**Claim:** $\kappa(K_{m,n}) = \min(m, n)$ (assume $m \leq n$).

### Step 1 — Upper bound via degrees

Every vertex in one partition has degree $n$; every vertex in the other has degree $m$. So $\delta(K_{m,n}) = m$.

### Step 2 — Apply Whitney

$$\kappa(K_{m,n}) \leq \delta(K_{m,n}) = m$$

### Step 3 — Show that removing $m - 1$ vertices cannot disconnect $K_{m,n}$

Any $m - 1$ vertices lie either:
- Entirely in the partition of size $n$ (with $m \leq n$), leaving at least one vertex in that partition, or
- Spread across both partitions, but since each partition originally has $m$ or $n$ vertices with $m \leq n$, at least one full partition remains.

If at least one full partition remains, every vertex in the surviving partition is still adjacent to every vertex in the other surviving partition. Hence the remaining graph is still connected (in fact, still a complete bipartite graph).

### Step 4 — Conclude

Removing $m - 1$ vertices is insufficient. Removing the entire $m$-partition disconnects the graph.

$$\boxed{\kappa(K_{m,n}) = m = \min(m, n)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Verifying Whitney's Inequality on the Petersen Graph

The **Petersen graph** $P$ has $|V| = 10$, is 3-regular, so $\delta(P) = 3$.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\delta(P)$ | $3$ |
| $\lambda(P)$ (edge connectivity) | $3$ |
| $\kappa(P)$ (vertex connectivity) | $3$ |

Check:
$$\kappa(P) = 3 \;\leq\; \lambda(P) = 3 \;\leq\; \delta(P) = 3 \;\; \checkmark$$

All three collapse to the same value — Petersen graph is **optimally robust**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Application of Menger's Theorem

**Problem:** In $G$, find the minimum vertex cut separating $u$ and $v$, given that there are **4 internally vertex-disjoint $u$–$v$ paths** by inspection.

By Menger's theorem:
$$\kappa_{u,v}(G) = \max(\text{internally disjoint } u\text{-}v \text{ paths}) = 4$$

Then the global connectivity satisfies:
$$\kappa(G) = \min_{u, v} \kappa_{u,v}(G)$$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Value / Bound |
|---|---|
| Minimum $\kappa(G)$ for connected graph | $1$ (if cut vertex exists) |
| Maximum $\kappa(G)$ for $n$-vertex graph | $n - 1$ (only $K_n$) |
| Relationship to $\delta(G)$ | $\kappa(G) \leq \delta(G)$ |
| Relationship to $\lambda(G)$ | $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$ |
| Trees | $\kappa = 1$ |
| Cycles | $\kappa = 2$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Graph with a Cut Vertex ($\kappa = 1$)

This diagram illustrates a graph where removing vertex **3** disconnects the structure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Vertex 1"] --- B["Vertex 2"]
    B --- C["Vertex 3"]
    C --- D["Vertex 4"]
    C --- E["Vertex 5"]
    A --- C
    F["Vertex 6"] --- G["Vertex 7"]
    G --- H["Vertex 8"]
    H --- F
    G --- C

    style C fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style G fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Reading the diagram:** Vertex **3** (highlighted in yellow) is the articulation point. Removing it splits the graph into two components: the cycle on the left and the triangle on the right. Hence $\kappa(G) = 1$.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Two Vertices Whose Removal Disconnects ($\kappa = 2$)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["A"] --- B1["B"]
    B1 --- C1["C"]
    C1 --- D1["D"]
    A1 --- E1["E"]
    E1 --- F1["F"]
    F1 --- G1["G"]
    C1 --- G1
    D1 --- H1["H"]
    H1 --- G1

    style B1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
    style C1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Removing vertices **B** and **C** simultaneously disconnects the path $A \to D$ from the cluster on the right. No single vertex removal disconnects the graph, so $\kappa(G) = 2$.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Block Diagram of Vertex Connectivity Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Input Graph G"] --> Step1["Step 1: Compute delta G = minimum degree"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Apply Whitney upper bound"]
    Step2 --> Step3{"Step 3: Does a cut vertex exist?"}
    Step3 -- Yes --> Result1["kappa G equals 1"]
    Step3 -- No --> Step4["Step 4: Test subsets of size 2"]
    Step4 --> Step5{"Step 5: Does any 2-vertex cut exist?"}
    Step5 -- Yes --> Result2["kappa G equals 2"]
    Step5 -- No --> Step6["Step 6: Use Menger theorem for higher k"]
    Step6 --> Result3["kappa G equals k"]

    style Start fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style Step3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Step5 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Result1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Result2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Result3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

**Reading the flowchart:** The diagram maps the algorithm used to determine $\kappa(G)$ for any arbitrary graph. KTU examiners often award partial credit for stating the decision logic clearly, even when a brute-force computation is infeasible.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set]**
**Define vertex connectivity of a graph. State Whitney's inequality.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertex Connectivity:** The vertex connectivity $\kappa(G)$ of a connected graph $G$ is the minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects $G$ or reduces it to a single vertex $K_1$.
>
> **Whitney's Inequality:** For any connected graph $G$,
> $$\kappa(G) \;\leq\; \lambda(G) \;\leq\; \delta(G)$$
> where $\lambda(G)$ is edge connectivity and $\delta(G)$ is the minimum vertex degree.
>
> A vertex whose removal disconnects $G$ is called a **cut vertex**; if such a vertex exists, then $\kappa(G) = 1$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Set]**
**What is a cut vertex? Show that every tree with $n \geq 2$ vertices has $\kappa(T) = 1$.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **cut vertex** (articulation point) is a vertex $v$ such that $G - v$ has more connected components than $G$.
>
> **Proof that every tree $T$ has $\kappa(T) = 1$:**
> 1. A tree with $n \geq 2$ has at least one **leaf vertex** $\ell$ with degree 1.
> 2. Remove $\ell$: the graph remains connected (because removing a leaf from a tree never disconnects it).
> 3. However, every tree with $n \geq 2$ has a **non-leaf vertex** of degree $\geq 2$ (an internal vertex on a longest path).
> 4. Removing any internal vertex of a tree **always** splits the tree into $\geq 2$ components (because a tree has no cycles).
> 5. Therefore, a cut vertex exists, and $\kappa(T) = 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose 2–4 marks by:
> 1. **Confusing $\kappa(G)$ with $\lambda(G)$** — Remember: $\kappa \leq \lambda$, they are NOT equal in general.
> 2. **Forgetting boundary cases** — $\kappa(K_1) = 0$ and $\kappa$ of a disconnected graph = $0$.
> 3. **Not justifying Whitney's inequality** — merely stating it earns only 1 mark; proving direction-wise takes 4 marks.
> 4. **Applying Menger's theorem to adjacent vertices** — the theorem strictly requires **non-adjacent** vertices.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** State and prove **Whitney's inequality** for a connected graph. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute $\kappa(G)$, $\lambda(G)$, and $\delta(G)$ for the following graph $G$ and verify Whitney's inequality. The graph has vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edges $\{12, 13, 14, 23, 35, 45\}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Proof of Whitney's Inequality:**

We must show two inequalities: $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$ and $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$.

**Part 1: $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$**

Let $v$ be a vertex of minimum degree $\delta(G)$. Each of its $\delta(G)$ incident edges has a distinct other endpoint (else a multi-edge would form, which we exclude in simple graphs). Removing these $\delta(G)$ edges disconnects $v$ from all its neighbors. Therefore, the minimum edge cut has size at most $\delta(G)$:
$$\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$

**Part 2: $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$**

Let $S$ be a minimum edge cut, $|S| = \lambda(G)$. Removing $S$ disconnects $G$ into at least two components $G_1, G_2$. Now consider the set $T$ of all **endpoints of edges in $S$** that lie in $G_1$. For each edge in $S$, at least one endpoint lies in $G_1$, so $|T| \leq |S| = \lambda(G)$. Removing $T$ from $G$ also disconnects $G$ because every edge between $G_1$ and $G_2$ is incident to some vertex in $T$.

Hence:
$$\kappa(G) \leq |T| \leq \lambda(G)$$

Combining both parts:
$$\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$

**(b) Computation for graph $G$:**

Adjacency list:
$$N(1) = \{2, 3, 4\}, \quad N(2) = \{1, 3\}, \quad N(3) = \{1, 2, 5\}, \quad N(4) = \{1, 5\}, \quad N(5) = \{3, 4\}$$

Degrees:
$$\deg(1) = 3, \quad \deg(2) = 2, \quad \deg(3) = 3, \quad \deg(4) = 2, \quad \deg(5) = 2$$

**Step 1:** $\delta(G) = \min(3, 2, 3, 2, 2) = 2$

**Step 2: Test $\kappa(G) = 1$.** Try removing vertex 3:
$G - 3$ has vertices $\{1, 2, 4, 5\}$ with edges $\{12, 14, 45\}$. The graph remains connected (path $2-1-4-5$). So vertex 3 is NOT a cut vertex.

Try removing vertex 1:
$G - 1$ has vertices $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with edges $\{23, 35, 45\}$. This forms a cycle $2-3-5-4$? No — edges are $\{23, 35, 45\}$. Vertex 4 is only connected to 5. So the structure is $2-3-5-4$, a path. The graph is still connected. Vertex 1 is NOT a cut vertex.

Try removing vertex 5:
$G - 5$ has vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with edges $\{12, 13, 14, 23\}$. This is connected.

Hence no cut vertex exists, so $\kappa(G) \geq 2$.

**Step 3:** Since $\kappa(G) \leq \delta(G) = 2$, we get $\kappa(G) = 2$.

**Step 4: Edge connectivity.** Remove edges incident to vertex 2 (edges $\{12, 23\}$): this disconnects vertex 2 from the rest. So $\lambda(G) \leq 2$. Trying single-edge removal: removing any one edge leaves the graph connected. Hence $\lambda(G) = 2$.

**Verification:**
$$\kappa(G) = 2 \leq \lambda(G) = 2 \leq \delta(G) = 2 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define vertex connectivity and edge connectivity. State and explain **Menger's theorem** (vertex version). (7 marks)

**(b)** For the complete bipartite graph $K_{3,4}$, compute $\kappa(G)$, $\lambda(G)$, and $\delta(G)$. Justify your answer using graph-theoretic properties. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Menger's Theorem:**

> **Definitions:**
> - **Vertex connectivity** $\kappa(G)$: minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects $G$.
> - **Edge connectivity** $\lambda(G)$: minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects $G$.
>
> **Menger's Theorem (Vertex Form):**
> Let $G$ be a connected graph and $u, v$ two **non-adjacent** vertices. Then the **maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $u$–$v$ paths** equals the **minimum number of vertices separating $u$ from $v$** (other than $u$ and $v$ themselves).
>
> **Significance:** This theorem is the cornerstone of network reliability theory. It tells us that if we want $k$ disjoint paths between $u$ and $v$ for fault tolerance, we must keep at least $k$ vertex-disjoint routes alive — equivalently, no set of $k - 1$ vertices can disconnect $u$ from $v$.

**(b) Computation for $K_{3,4}$:**

Partition $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$, $B = \{b_1, b_2, b_3, b_4\}$. Every $a_i$ connects to every $b_j$.

**Step 1: Degree sequence.**
- Each $a_i$ has degree $|B| = 4$.
- Each $b_j$ has degree $|A| = 3$.

Hence $\delta(K_{3,4}) = 3$.

**Step 2: Vertex connectivity.**

By Whitney: $\kappa(G) \leq \delta(G) = 3$.

To show $\kappa(K_{3,4}) \geq 3$: Removing any 2 vertices can at most reduce one partition. Cases:
- Both removed from $A$: partition $A$ still has 1 vertex, partition $B$ unchanged → still complete bipartite → connected.
- Both removed from $B$: partition $B$ still has 2 vertices, partition $A$ unchanged → still complete bipartite → connected.
- One from each: both partitions still non-empty → still complete bipartite → connected.

So removing any 2 vertices leaves the graph connected. Hence $\kappa(K_{3,4}) \geq 3$.

Removing all 3 vertices of $A$: graph collapses → disconnected (or trivial).

Therefore $\kappa(K_{3,4}) = 3 = \min(3, 4)$.

**Step 3: Edge connectivity.**

By Whitney: $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G) = 3$.

For each vertex in $A$, removing all 3 incident edges isolates that vertex. So $\lambda(G) \leq 3$. Testing smaller cuts: removing 2 edges cannot isolate any vertex (every vertex still has at least 1 or 2 edges). So $\lambda(G) = 3$.

**Verification (Whitney):**
$$\kappa(K_{3,4}) = 3 \;\leq\; \lambda(K_{3,4}) = 3 \;\leq\; \delta(K_{3,4}) = 3 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Vertex connectivity $\kappa(G)$** = minimum vertex cut size; $K_1$, disconnected graphs have $\kappa = 0$.
- ✅ **Cut vertex** = vertex cut of size 1; its existence implies $\kappa(G) = 1$.
- ✅ **Whitney's inequality** $\kappa \leq \lambda \leq \delta$ is the **single most-tested formula** in this module.
- ✅ **Menger's theorem** applies only to **non-adjacent** vertices — common trap.
- ✅ **$\kappa(K_n) = n - 1$**, $\kappa(K_{m,n}) = \min(m, n)$.
- ✅ **Trees:** $\kappa = 1$ (always); **Cycles:** $\kappa = 2$ (always for $n \geq 3$).
- ✅ **Petersen graph and $n$-cube $Q_n$** are classically $\kappa = 3$ and $\kappa = n$ respectively.
- ✅ For any graph $G$: $\kappa(G) \leq |V(G)| - 1$.
- ✅ Always **state Whitney's inequality in both directions** with justification to earn full marks.
- ✅ In exam answers, **draw the graph** before computing $\kappa$ — visualization earns partial credit even if computation is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT523 - GRAPH THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Edge Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Connectivity (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
>
> The **edge connectivity** of a connected graph $G$, denoted by $\lambda(G)$ (also written as $\kappa'(G)$), is the **minimum number of edges** whose removal from $G$ results in a graph that is either **disconnected** or becomes a **trivial graph** (a single vertex or an isolated graph).

Formally, for a connected graph $G = (V, E)$:

$$\lambda(G) = \min_{S \subseteq E} \left\{ \vert S \vert \; : \; G - S \text{ is disconnected or trivial} \right\}$$

The set $S$ of edges whose removal disconnects the graph is called an **edge cut** (or **cut-set**). Each minimal edge cut must contain at least one edge from every path between the two partitioned components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Isolated Vertices**
>
> If $G$ is a disconnected graph or contains isolated vertices, then by convention, $\lambda(G) = 0$. In KTU examinations, this convention is rarely tested but must be mentioned for completeness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Weakness" Principle

Imagine a city road network where each road is an edge and each intersection is a vertex. **Edge connectivity answers the question**: *"What is the smallest number of roads we must block (e.g., for repairs or disasters) before the city falls apart into two unreachable zones?"*

- **$\lambda(G) = 1$**: There exists a **single road** whose closure disconnects the city. This is a classic *bridge* (cut-edge).
- **$\lambda(G) = 2$**: No single road is critical, but there exists a *pair* of roads whose simultaneous closure disconnects the network.
- **$\lambda(G) = k$**: The network can survive the failure of any $(k-1)$ roads, but no more.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition**
>
> In **reliable network design** (telecommunication grids, power distribution systems, computer clusters), a higher $\lambda(G)$ indicates a **more fault-tolerant topology**. The KTU syllabus treats edge connectivity as a fundamental metric for **network robustness analysis**.

## 1.3 Notation Standard

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\lambda(G)$ | Edge connectivity of $G$ |
| $\kappa(G)$ | Vertex connectivity of $G$ |
| $\delta(G)$ | Minimum vertex degree |
| $E(G)$ | Edge set of $G$ |
| $V(G)$ | Vertex set of $G$ |
| $n$ | Order of the graph ($\vert V(G) \vert$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Hierarchy — Whitney's Inequality

The single most tested relationship in this topic is the **Whitney Inequality**, which links three fundamental connectivity parameters:

$$\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$

Where:
- $\kappa(G)$ = **Vertex Connectivity** (minimum vertices to remove to disconnect)
- $\lambda(G)$ = **Edge Connectivity** (minimum edges to remove to disconnect)
- $\delta(G)$ = **Minimum Degree** of the graph

### Interpretation of the Hierarchy

- $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$: The vertex with the smallest degree has exactly $\delta(G)$ incident edges. Removing all of them isolates that vertex, giving an edge cut of size $\delta(G)$.
- $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$: Removing $\lambda(G)$ edges adjacent to a particular vertex isolates it. Removing that one vertex also disconnects, hence vertex connectivity is at most edge connectivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whitney's Theorem (1932)**
>
> For every graph $G$ with at least 3 vertices, the inequality $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ holds, and **equality holds throughout** if and only if $G$ is **3-connected** (i.e., $\kappa(G) \geq 3$) or $G$ is a complete graph or a cycle.

## 2.2 Special Graph Classes — Closed-Form Results

KTU examinations frequently test the edge connectivity of standard graph families. The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**:

| Graph Type | Description | Edge Connectivity $\lambda(G)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Complete Graph** $K_n$ | Every pair of vertices is adjacent | $n - 1$ |
| **Complete Bipartite** $K_{m,n}$ ($m \leq n$) | Bipartition of sizes $m$ and $n$ | $m$ |
| **Cycle Graph** $C_n$ ($n \geq 3$) | Vertices arranged in a cycle | $2$ |
| **Path Graph** $P_n$ ($n \geq 2$) | Vertices in a linear chain | $1$ |
| **Wheel Graph** $W_n$ | $C_{n-1}$ plus central vertex | $3$ (for $n \geq 4$) |
| **Petersen Graph** | Famous 3-regular graph | $3$ |
| **Tree with $\geq 2$ vertices** | Acyclic connected graph | $1$ |
| **Disconnected Graph** | At least 2 components | $0$ |

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — Edge Connectivity Master Table

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are tested in KTU University Examinations. Memorize the table before attempting problems.**

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Whitney's Inequality** | $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ |
| 2 | **Complete Graph Edge Connectivity** | $\lambda(K_n) = n - 1$ |
| 3 | **Complete Bipartite Edge Connectivity** | $\lambda(K_{m,n}) = \min(m, n)$ |
| 4 | **Cycle Edge Connectivity** | $\lambda(C_n) = 2$ |
| 5 | **Wheel Edge Connectivity** | $\lambda(W_n) = 3$ |
| 6 | **Trivial Graph** | $\lambda(K_1) = 0$ |
| 7 | **Empty Graph on $n \geq 2$ vertices** | $\lambda(\overline{K_n}) = 0$ |
| 8 | **Max-Flow Min-Cut (s-t version)** | $\lambda(G) = \min$ edge-disjoint path pairs between any two vertices |
| 9 | **Menger's Theorem (Edge Version)** | $\lambda(G) = $ max number of edge-disjoint paths between any pair of vertices |
| 10 | **Upper Bound via Degree** | $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
>
> - **Computer Networks**: $\lambda(G)$ determines the minimum number of cable failures that can isolate a node in a LAN topology.
> - **Power Grids**: Used to design redundant transmission lines so that single-line failures do not cause blackouts.
> - **Transportation Engineering**: Models the resilience of highway networks to road closures.
> - **VLSI Circuit Design**: Edge connectivity helps route redundant interconnects in fault-tolerant chip layouts.

## 2.4 Logical Steps to Determine $\lambda(G)$ in General

For an arbitrary graph, follow this KTU-validated algorithm:

1. **Check for trivial cases**: If $G$ is disconnected or has $\leq 1$ vertex, $\lambda(G) = 0$.
2. **Compute $\delta(G)$**: Find the smallest degree. This gives the upper bound.
3. **Identify all minimal edge cuts** (separating sets $S \subseteq V(G)$): For each non-trivial partition, count crossing edges.
4. **Apply Menger's Theorem (Edge Version)**: For every pair of vertices $(u, v)$, find the maximum number of edge-disjoint $u$–$v$ paths. The minimum over all pairs equals $\lambda(G)$.
5. **Choose the minimum** edge cut size across all partitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Whitney's Inequality — Component 1: $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$

**Goal:** Show that removing all edges incident to a minimum-degree vertex yields a valid edge cut.

Let $v$ be a vertex of $G$ with $\deg(v) = \delta(G)$. Consider the set of edges:

$$S = \{ e \in E(G) \; : \; e \text{ is incident with } v \}$$

The cardinality of $S$ is:

$$\vert S \vert = \deg(v) = \delta(G)$$

Now consider $G - S$. Since every edge incident to $v$ is removed, the vertex $v$ becomes **isolated** in $G - S$. Provided $G$ has at least two vertices, this means:

$$G - S \text{ is disconnected (or trivial)}$$

Therefore $S$ is a valid edge cut, and by the definition of edge connectivity:

$$\lambda(G) \leq \vert S \vert = \delta(G)$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Whitney's Inequality — Component 2: $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$

**Goal:** Show that vertex connectivity is bounded above by edge connectivity.

Let $S^*$ be a minimum edge cut of $G$, so $\vert S^* \vert = \lambda(G)$. After removing $S^*$, the graph $G - S^*$ becomes disconnected. Suppose the resulting components are $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$ with $k \geq 2$.

**Case A:** $k \geq 3$. Then removing any single vertex $u \in C_2$ leaves $C_1, \ldots, C_{i-1}, C_{i+1}, \ldots, C_k$ and the rest of $C_2 \setminus \{u\}$ — still disconnected. So:

$$\kappa(G) \leq 1 \leq \lambda(G)$$

(since $\lambda(G) \geq 1$ for connected graphs with $\geq 2$ vertices).

**Case B:** $k = 2$. Pick a vertex $v \in C_1$. Removing $v$ may or may not disconnect the graph further; if it does, then $\kappa(G) = 1 \leq \lambda(G)$. 

A tighter argument: **contract** all edges of $C_1$ except one into a single super-vertex, and do the same for $C_2$. The cut between the two super-vertices contains exactly $\lambda(G)$ edges, and each super-vertex has degree at least $\lambda(G)$. By the symmetric construction, vertex connectivity is at most edge connectivity.

$$\boxed{\therefore \; \kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Edge Connectivity of a Pentagon with One Diagonal

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
Find the edge connectivity of the graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edge set:

$$E = \{ (1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5), (5,1), (1,3) \}$$

This is a pentagon cycle $C_5$ with one diagonal $(1, 3)$ added.

### Step 1 — Compute the Minimum Degree $\delta(G)$

The degrees of each vertex are:

$$\deg(1) = 3, \quad \deg(2) = 2, \quad \deg(3) = 3, \quad \deg(4) = 2, \quad \deg(5) = 2$$

Therefore:

$$\delta(G) = 2$$

So by Whitney's inequality:

$$\lambda(G) \leq 2$$

### Step 2 — Enumerate All Minimal Edge Cuts

For each vertex $v$, we count the edges incident to it (each gives an edge cut that isolates $v$):

**Cut around vertex 2:**

$$S_2 = \{ (1,2), (2,3) \} \implies \vert S_2 \vert = 2$$

**Cut around vertex 4:**

$$S_4 = \{ (3,4), (4,5) \} \implies \vert S_4 \vert = 2$$

**Cut around vertex 5:**

$$S_5 = \{ (4,5), (5,1) \} \implies \vert S_5 \vert = 2$$

**Cut around vertex 1:**

$$S_1 = \{ (1,2), (1,3), (5,1) \} \implies \vert S_1 \vert = 3$$

**Cut around vertex 3:**

$$S_3 = \{ (1,3), (2,3), (3,4) \} \implies \vert S_3 \vert = 3$$

### Step 3 — Partition-Based Cuts

Beyond single-vertex cuts, consider partitions $\{A, V \setminus A\}$:

**Partition $A = \{1, 2\}$:**

Crossing edges: $(1,3), (2,3), (5,1) \to 3$ edges.

**Partition $A = \{1, 5\}$:**

Crossing edges: $(1,2), (1,3), (4,5) \to 3$ edges.

**Partition $A = \{2, 3\}$:**

Crossing edges: $(1,2), (3,4), (1,3) \to 3$ edges.

### Step 4 — Take the Minimum

The minimum edge cut size across all partitions is:

$$\lambda(G) = \min\{ 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, \ldots \} = 2$$

### Step 5 — Verification via Menger's Theorem (Edge Version)

Pick vertices $u = 2$ and $v = 4$. Find the maximum number of edge-disjoint paths between them:

- $P_1: 2 \to 1 \to 5 \to 4$ — uses edges $(1,2), (5,1), (4,5)$
- $P_2: 2 \to 3 \to 4$ — uses edges $(2,3), (3,4)$

These two paths share no edges. We cannot find a third edge-disjoint path because vertex 2 has only 2 incident edges. Hence:

$$\lambda(G) = 2 = \text{(max edge-disjoint paths between } 2 \text{ and } 4\text{)}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\lambda(G) = 2}$$

### Step 6 — Cross-Check Whitney's Inequality

For our graph: $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ becomes:

$$\kappa(G) \leq 2 \leq 2$$

The vertex connectivity is also 2 (removing vertices 1 and 3 disconnects the graph). Therefore $\kappa(G) = \lambda(G) = \delta(G) = 2$, satisfying Whitney's equality condition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Pentagon with Diagonal (Worked Example)

The following Mermaid block renders the graph from Section 3.3, with each edge labeled by its identifier for traceability.

```mermaid

    A1[Vertex 1] --- E12["e12: 1-2"]
    A2[Vertex 2] --- E12
    A2 --- E23["e23: 2-3"]
    A3[Vertex 3] --- E23
    A3 --- E34["e34: 3-4"]
    A4[Vertex 4] --- E34
    A4 --- E45["e45: 4-5"]
    A5[Vertex 5] --- E45
    A5 --- E51["e51: 5-1"]
    A1 --- E51
    A1 --- E13["e13: 1-3 diagonal"]
    A3 --- E13

    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **pink vertices** (2, 4, 5) are the minimum-degree vertices ($\delta = 2$).
- The **peach vertices** (1, 3) are higher-degree vertices ($\deg = 3$).
- The **diagonal edge** $e13$ is the only edge connecting vertices of higher degree.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Edge Cut Visualisation Around Vertex 2

The following block diagram depicts the process of isolating vertex 2 by removing its incident edges.

```mermaid

    subgraph Before["BEFORE CUT: G intact"]
        direction LR
        B1[V1] --- B2[V2]
        B1 --- B3[V3]

        B4 --- B5[V5]
        B5 --- B1
    end

    subgraph Cutset["EDGE CUT S2 = e12, e23"]
        direction TB
        CT1["Remove edge e12 connecting V1-V2"]
        CT2["Remove edge e23 connecting V2-V3"]
        CT3["Both edges share vertex V2"]
    end

    subgraph After["AFTER CUT: V2 isolated"]
        direction LR
        A1[V1] --- A3[V3]
        A3 --- A4[V4]
        A4 --- A5[V5]
        A5 --- A1
        ISO[V2 ISOLATED]:::iso
    end

    Before --> Cutset --> After

    classDef iso fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture Flow for Edge Connectivity Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST["Step 0: Receive Graph G"] --> S1{"Is G disconnected?"}
    S1 -- Yes --> R0["Output: lambda G = 0"]
    S1 -- No --> S2{"Has G only 1 vertex?"}
    S2 -- Yes --> R1["Output: lambda G = 0"]
    S2 -- No --> S3["Compute minimum degree delta G"]
    S3 --> S4["Upper bound: lambda G is at most delta G"]
    S4 --> S5["Enumerate all partitions A and V minus A"]
    S5 --> S6["For each partition, count crossing edges"]
    S6 --> S7["Find the minimum crossing edge count"]
    S7 --> S8["Verify with Menger edge theorem"]
    S8 --> RFinal["Output: lambda G equals minimum cut size"]

    style ST fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff,stroke:#333
    style R0 fill:#D0021B,color:#fff

```

**Reading the architecture diagram:**
- The flowchart describes the **systematic decision tree** that a student should follow when solving KTU problems.
- Each colored box represents a *decision stage* or *computational step*.
- Green terminal nodes represent **final output values**.
- Red terminal nodes are **trivial-case exits**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define edge connectivity of a graph. Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
The edge connectivity of a connected graph $G$, denoted by $\lambda(G)$, is the minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects $G$ or reduces it to a trivial graph.

**Example:** For the cycle $C_4$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,1)\}$, removing any single edge leaves a path $P_4$ which is still connected. However, removing two non-adjacent edges such as $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$ results in two disjoint paths $P_2$ and $P_2$. Hence $\lambda(C_4) = 2$.

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**State and explain Whitney's inequality relating vertex connectivity, edge connectivity, and minimum degree.**

**Model Answer:**
Whitney's inequality states that for any connected graph $G$:

$$\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$

where $\kappa(G)$ is the vertex connectivity, $\lambda(G)$ is the edge connectivity, and $\delta(G)$ is the minimum vertex degree. The inequality $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ holds because removing all edges incident to the minimum-degree vertex isolates it. The inequality $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$ holds because any minimum edge cut can be converted to a vertex separator by removing at most one vertex from each resulting component.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define edge connectivity. Find $\lambda(G)$ for the graph $G$ with $V(G) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and $E(G) = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (3,4), (4,5), (5,6), (6,4)\}$. Justify each step.

**(b)** [7 Marks] State Whitney's inequality. Verify it for the graph $G$ in part (a), and for $K_4$ (complete graph on 4 vertices).

#### Model Solution to B-A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

Graph $G$ consists of two triangles sharing only the vertex $3$ (one triangle on $\{1, 2, 3\}$ and another on $\{4, 5, 6\}$ joined via $3$). Wait — re-reading, the second triangle has edges $\{(3,4), (4,5), (5,6), (6,4)\}$, which is a cycle $C_4$ on vertices $\{3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

The structure is: **Triangle $T_1$** $= \{1, 2, 3\}$ with edges $(1,2), (2,3), (3,1)$, and **Cycle $C$** $= \{3, 4, 5, 6\}$ with edges $(3,4), (4,5), (5,6), (6,4)$, sharing vertex $3$.

**Degrees:**

$$\deg(1) = 2, \quad \deg(2) = 2, \quad \deg(3) = 4, \quad \deg(4) = 2, \quad \deg(5) = 2, \quad \deg(6) = 2$$

$$\delta(G) = 2 \implies \lambda(G) \leq 2$$

**Edge cuts by vertex isolation:**

- Isolate vertex $1$: cut $\{(1,2), (3,1)\}$ — size $2$.
- Isolate vertex $2$: cut $\{(1,2), (2,3)\}$ — size $2$.
- Isolate vertex $4$: cut $\{(3,4), (6,4)\}$ — size $2$.
- Isolate vertex $5$: cut $\{(4,5), (5,6)\}$ — size $2$.
- Isolate vertex $6$: cut $\{(5,6), (6,4)\}$ — size $2$.

**Partition cuts:**

- $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$: crossing edges $= (3,4)$ — size $1$. **This is smaller!**

Wait — this is critical. Removing edge $(3,4)$ alone disconnects the graph! Hence:

$$\lambda(G) = 1$$

**Verification:** Edge $(3,4)$ is a **bridge** (cut-edge). Removal leaves the triangle $\{1, 2, 3\}$ disconnected from the path $\{4, 5, 6\}$ cycle's remnant.

**Final Answer:** $\lambda(G) = 1$.

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Whitney's Inequality:** $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$.

**For graph $G$ from part (a):**
- $\delta(G) = 2$
- $\lambda(G) = 1$
- $\kappa(G) = 1$ (vertex 3 is an articulation point)
- Verification: $1 \leq 1 \leq 2$ ✓

**For $K_4$:**
